entry.title: The argument framework is a flexible approach to evidence in healthcare
entry.content:
The argument framework places heterogeneous evidence in an auditable chain of reasoning: data support or defeat premises, and premises jointly support a clinical conclusion. It exposes inferential gaps, but the article supplies neither a reproducible weighting algorithm nor evidence that the framework improves decision accuracy.

The authors

Jonathan Fuller, Benjamin Chin-Yee, and Ross E. G. Upshur are affiliated with the University of Pittsburgh, Western University, the University of Cambridge, and the University of Toronto. Their backgrounds span philosophy of medicine, hematology, and public health. This four-page 2024 Nature Medicine Comment proposes a conceptual framework for evidence appraisal.

The problem

Conventional evidence-based medicine works well for bounded questions answered by several similar randomized trials, such as the average effect of an intervention in a defined population. When decisions also depend on single-arm trials, surrogate outcomes, observational data, mechanistic experiments, or models, pooling comparable effect estimates cannot show how those sources jointly support a conclusion.

The authors call the conventional approach the measurement framework: therapeutic evidence consists mainly of comparative measurements of intervention effects, quality is judged through validity, bias, and imprecision, and meta-analysis is the main synthesis tool. The proposed argument framework begins with a therapeutic hypothesis, identifies the premises needed for it, and finds data that support or defeat each premise.

The approach

The article reports no systematic search or empirical study. Drawing on logic, statistics, and epistemology, it presents nodal argument diagrams. Conclusions, premises, and evidence are nodes; arrows encode inferential support or defeat; brackets mark premises that must work jointly; minus signs mark defeaters; and thicker links may indicate stronger support.

The evaluation object is a complete argument made of evidence nodes, premises, and a conclusion. It gains support when premises have sound data, inferential links are defensible, and major defeaters have been addressed. The practical effect is to alter how strongly the conclusion is warranted and to locate disagreement in data, premises, or inference. The article specifies no common score or upgrade threshold.

These diagrams resemble causal DAGs visually but have different semantics. Argument nodes can be data, propositions, or hypotheses, and arrows represent epistemic support. Causal-DAG nodes are usually variables and arrows encode assumed causal effects. An argument diagram cannot directly supply d-separation, a backdoor criterion, or an adjustment set.

Key results

The paper gives two illustrations and no new clinical effect estimates. In precision oncology, meaningful overall-survival improvement is the conclusion (H_C). An observed objective response rate from a single-arm trial (O), the premise that response predicts survival (P), and absence of concerning safety signals (S) must jointly hold. Statistical validation, biological validation, natural regression, and later safety data support or weaken those premises.

PI3K inhibitors illustrate defeat. Several indications received accelerated approval on single-arm response rates, while later randomized trials reported lethal toxicities and excess deaths, leading to withdrawal of those indications. These findings weaken the premise that response rate predicts survival benefit for this drug-disease setting.

The second illustration concerns community mask wearing by asymptomatic people. Its diagram accommodates transmission biology, particle-blocking experiments, asymptomatic transmission, observational studies, randomized trials, and epidemic models. The example shows that a population-level intervention requires several inferential steps, only some of which appear in one meta-analysis.

Table 1 compares the frameworks by evidence definition, eligible study types, appraisal aim, synthesis tool, and useful setting. A forest plot represents measurement synthesis; an argument diagram represents argumentation synthesis. Measurement results can still enter the latter as evidence nodes.

How solid is the evidence

This is a conceptual Comment grounded in prior philosophy and two selected examples. It demonstrates expressive capacity, not superiority. There is no inter-rater study of diagram construction and no comparison showing better guideline, regulatory, or clinical decisions.

Argument strength remains operationally underspecified. Link thickness, joint premises, and defeaters can be shown qualitatively, but there are no rules for combining conflicting evidence, controlling review overlap, handling shared bias, or preventing one dataset from supporting several nodes repeatedly. Bayesian-network interpretation could quantify confirmation, but prior probabilities and dependence structures would still require separate modeling.

The description of the measurement framework is simplified. GRADE and contemporary appraisal are not confined to randomized trials or meta-analysis; they can address indirectness, dose response, confounding, and diverse designs. The argument framework is best used as an explicit reasoning layer, while risk-of-bias appraisal, effect estimation, and transportability remain necessary.

entry.title: Secondhand smoke exposure and human health: an umbrella review
entry.content:
This umbrella review organizes secondhand-smoke evidence into 226 exposure-outcome associations. Lung cancer, cardiovascular disease, and selected perinatal outcomes have comparatively strong statistical credibility, while GRADE certainty remains low or very low for most results. The review does not establish that every reported association is causal.

The authors

Shengxin Zhang, Shen Li, Xinggang Yang, and colleagues are affiliated with West China Hospital of Sichuan University, the University of Electronic Science and Technology of China, and other institutions. Hu Liao, Juan Huang, and Xuelei Ma are corresponding authors. The paper appeared in Nature Human Behaviour in July 2026. Its protocol was registered in PROSPERO (CRD42024583424), and the data and analysis code are available through Zenodo.

The problem

Secondhand-smoke research spans pregnancy, childhood, and adulthood, with substantial variation in sources, measurements, and outcomes. Multiple systematic reviews may repeatedly include the same primary studies. The authors sought to rerun existing meta-analyses under one protocol and distinguish mature evidence, useful research gaps, and questions where adding similar observational studies may offer little information.

The exposure scope needs to be separated before interpretation. Environmental secondhand smoke in adults or children concerns non-active smokers exposed at home, work, or elsewhere. Prenatal maternal smoking treats the fetus as the passively exposed individual. Maternal active smoking, transplacental fetal exposure, and postnatal household exposure are different estimands.

The approach

Four databases were searched through 24 August 2025, yielding 5,405 records. Of 299 systematic reviews eligible for reanalysis, representative reviews were selected for each exposure-outcome combination by dose-response reporting, study count, methodological quality, and exposure detail. The final reanalysis used study-level data from 111 reviews.

Each association was repooled with a REML random-effects model. ORs, RRs, and HRs remained on their original scales. The authors assessed AMSTAR 2, GRADE, 95% prediction intervals, heterogeneity, small-study effects, excess significance, and Rosenthal fail-safe numbers. For non-significant results, scenario analyses estimated how many additional studies would be needed for significance or 80% power.

The object of evidence classification is one exposure-outcome meta-analysis. Class I is triggered by more than 1,000 cases, or more than 20,000 participants for continuous outcomes; random-effects P<10^-6; a prediction interval excluding the null; I²<50%; and no small-study effect or excess significance. Its practical effect is placement in the highest statistical-credibility tier. It does not remove residual confounding, exposure misclassification, or selection bias.

Key results

The reanalysis covered 111 systematic reviews, about 253.3 million participants, 130 health outcomes, and 226 independent associations. Eighty-seven outcomes had at least one significant association, and 28 outcome findings reached Class I or II. Every Class I/II result exceeded the fail-safe-number threshold of 5k+10, although that metric only counts hypothetical null-effect studies needed to change statistical significance.

For adult chronic disease, secondhand-smoke exposure was associated with lung cancer at RR 1.24 (95% CI 1.16–1.32; 82 studies; Class II; low GRADE certainty), overall cardiovascular disease at OR 1.24 (95% CI 1.16–1.31; 52 studies; Class II; very-low GRADE certainty), and stroke at RR 1.24 (95% CI 1.16–1.32; 38 studies; Class II). Lung cancer also showed dose-response patterns by intensity, duration, and cumulative exposure.

Cancer evidence varied sharply. Breast cancer had RR 1.25 (Class III), cervical cancer RR 1.53 (Class III), pancreatic cancer RR 1.20 (Class III), and colorectal cancer RR 1.17 (Class IV). Kidney and endometrial cancer estimates were not statistically significant. Beyond lung cancer, most cancer associations had very-low GRADE certainty or weaker statistical classes and are better treated as candidates for triangulation.

For early-life outcomes, prenatal maternal smoking of 11-19 cigarettes per day was associated with low birth weight at OR 2.05 (Class I), while prenatal maternal smoking was associated with perinatal death at RR 1.33 (Class II). These estimates concern fetal passive exposure to maternal active smoking and should not be transferred directly to environmental secondhand smoke among non-smoking pregnant women.

How solid is the evidence

Repooling study-level effects under one model reduces incomparability from copied review-level summaries. Overlap analysis, preregistration, duplicate extraction, and open data and code improve auditability. Reporting Evidence class beside GRADE also makes visible the gap between strong statistical signals and limited causal certainty.

All source evidence is observational. Residual active smoking, socioeconomic position, occupational exposure, and correlated behaviors can confound associations. Most studies measured exposure by questionnaire, leaving recall error and former-smoker misclassification. An umbrella review inherits selective reporting and bias from source reviews. Selecting one representative review reduces duplicate counting but may discard distinct time windows or population definitions.

The reported P values were not adjusted for multiple comparisons. A large fail-safe number assumes added studies have an average null effect and cannot rule out systematic bias. Prospective scenario analysis depends on assumed future effects and variances; it measures sensitivity to more studies of the same kind, not the value of information or causal identifiability.

entry.title: Construction Specification of Directed Acyclic Graphs: An Introduction to the ESC-DAGs Method and Case Interpretation in Cerebrovascular Disease Research
entry.content:
This Chinese case study walks through ESC-DAGs and identifies the focal-search paradox and edge-by-edge workload, helping clinical teams calibrate expectations before implementation.

The authors

郝允逸 and 赵性泉 are based at the Neurology Center of Beijing Tiantan Hospital, Capital Medical University. 夏雪, 胥芹, and 王安心 are affiliated with the Beijing Neurosurgical Institute and the Department of Clinical Epidemiology and Clinical Trials at Capital Medical University. The group works in stroke and clinical epidemiology.

The problem

Clinical researchers know that DAGs can guide confounder selection but have few Chinese, stepwise examples grounded in stroke research. The article introduces ESC-DAGs and uses baseline or admission serum uric acid and functional prognosis after ischemic stroke to show how regression papers become graphs, edges are audited, and graphs are integrated.

The approach

Mapping converts each paper's exposure, outcome, and controls into a saturated implied graph. Translation reviews temporality, face validity, theory, and a potential-outcomes thought experiment for both directions of every edge. Integration adds retained edges to a blank graph, reviews newly possible links, and may group nodes with similar inputs and outputs under a broader construct.

The stroke example searches PubMed for serum uric acid and functional outcome and shows how two studies create implied graphs. A variable adjusted in a paper is not automatically accepted as a confounder; reviewers must justify temporal order and whether setting alternative values would change the outcome.

Key results

This is a methods tutorial without a new cohort effect estimate. Figure 1 explains chains, forks, and colliders; Table 1 lists the three ESC-DAGs stages; Figures 4–6 show implied graphs, translated DAGs, and integration for the uric-acid example. Some age and sex nodes are combined into a demographic construct for readability.

The authors identify two operational problems. Searching only the focal exposure–outcome relation can miss other relations defining confounding structure. Mapping every paper and translating each edge in both directions is labor-intensive. They suggest broader searches for causes of exposure and outcome and, in complex projects, summarizing variables before joint processing.

How solid is the evidence

As a secondary methods article, it faithfully explains the original ESC-DAGs workflow and offers a locally relevant example. It expands only a few papers, performs no complete review or final effect analysis, and does not show that the proposed shortcut is equivalent to the original process. Edge decisions still depend on timing and clinical knowledge.

Combining specific nodes improves readability but can hide different time windows and pathways. Serum uric acid may reflect kidney function, metabolic status, or acute stress; an imprecise node changes the meaning of the adjustment set.

entry.title: Constructing causal pathways for premature cardiovascular disease mortality using directed acyclic graphs with integrating evidence synthesis and expert knowledge
entry.content:
Evidence synthesis, Fuzzy Delphi consensus, and a real-data demonstration form a three-stage workflow; expert–model agreement supports feasibility, not validation of a true causal graph.

The authors

Wan Shakira Rodzlan Hasani, Kamarul Imran Musa, and Kueh Yee Cheng are affiliated with Universiti Sains Malaysia; Xin Wee Chen with Universiti Teknologi MARA. The team covers community medicine, public health, and biostatistics and uses linked Malaysian national health survey and mortality data.

The problem

ESC-DAGs can generate an initial graph from literature, but experts often disagree on omitted nodes and edge directions. Using premature cardiovascular mortality, the authors create a structured expert-revision and consensus rule, then examine how feasible consensus graphs behave with available survey data.

The approach

Phase 1 builds an initial ESC-DAG. Phase 2 interviews 12 public-health, epidemiology, and clinical experts separately. Eleven experts then rate 80 causal statements on a seven-point scale. The Fuzzy Delphi object is each candidate path. A threshold d≤0.2 and agreement ≥75% trigger retention; the practical effect is keeping, removing, or renaming an edge.

Phase 3 proposes three data-feasible DAGs for total effects of diabetes, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia on premature cardiovascular mortality. Experts rate validity, feasibility, and adjustment sets. Logistic regression compares AIC, AUC, pseudo-R², and classification metrics.

Key results

Of 80 second-round statements, 35 paths fail the joint agreement and d thresholds, leaving 45 consensus paths. Experts rank Model 1 as most feasible. Model 3 ranks first for structural validity and adjustment-set agreement, with 91.67% agreement on validity.

Model 3 has the lowest AIC (3,903.22) and highest AUC (82.02%), versus 81.46% for Model 1. Model 1 has slightly higher accuracy, 76.31% versus 75.95%. Diagnosed diabetes has an OR of about 3.30–3.53 across the three adjustments.

How solid is the evidence

The study quantifies disagreement and records edge-removal reasons, offering more transparency than an informal panel. The panel is small, with 11 second-round respondents. Agreement and d measure concentration of opinion, not truth, and shared context may limit external validity.

AUC, AIC, and accuracy are predictive or fit metrics. Model 3's AUC advantage is only 0.46–0.56 points and its accuracy is slightly lower; none validates causal structure. Key survey variables are self-reported, and simplifying the graph to measured nodes can leave residual confounding and overfit the setting.

entry.title: Directed acyclic graphs in choosing covariates for multivariable model of observational study
entry.content:
This Chinese review places data-driven covariate-selection errors, DAG path rules, and clinical adjustment decisions in one framework, making it a useful applied introduction.

The authors

曹雨滋, 袁驰, and 李舍予 are affiliated with Endocrinology and the MAGIC China Center at West China Hospital, Sichuan University; 刘刚 with Tongji Medical College, Huazhong University of Science and Technology; 孙凤 with Epidemiology and Biostatistics at Peking University. The team spans clinical endocrinology, evidence-based medicine, and epidemiology.

The problem

Observational studies often select covariates using stepwise regression, univariable P values, or change-in-estimate rules. These association-driven procedures can miss common causes or condition on mediators, instruments, and colliders. The review explains how DAGs separate a variable's causal role from statistical association.

The approach

The article distinguishes confounders, effect modifiers, mediators, instruments, colliders, and descendants, then compares forward selection, backward elimination, the 10% change-in-estimate rule, and high-dimensional propensity scores. A type 2 diabetes–fracture example shows why statistical significance or estimate change does not establish adjustment validity.

Graph development follows ESC-DAGs: map study claims, review edges through temporality, face validity, theory, and counterfactual reasoning, then integrate. The adjustment object is every open noncausal exposure–outcome path. The backdoor criterion triggers blocking of all biasing paths; the practical effect is a minimal sufficient set excluding mediators, colliders, and unnecessary instruments.

Key results

This is a narrative methods review without new effect estimates. It summarizes four data-driven procedures, DAG path-opening rules, and alternatives including common-cause, pretreatment-variable, and disjunctive-cause criteria. DAGitty can calculate minimal sufficient sets and test whether a proposed set blocks all biasing paths.

One example reports an adjusted OR of 0.80 after a statistically selected variable is included, yet that variable may be a collider; estimate change alone is not evidence for adjustment. The review also notes that roughly two-thirds of applied DAG studies explicitly displayed their graph, with fewer reporting construction assumptions systematically.

How solid is the evidence

The article clearly synthesizes classic DAG logic and recent applied recommendations for a Chinese clinical audience. It is not a systematic review and provides no simulation or empirical comparison of selection strategies. Numerical examples and prevalence claims still depend on the cited sources.

A DAG determines adjustment under an assumed structure but does not quantify confounding. An incomplete graph yields a wrong set, and multiple valid sets cannot identify the correct graph from data alone. Measurement error and unmeasured confounding require separate sensitivity analyses.

entry.title: A case study and proposal for publishing directed acyclic graphs: The effectiveness of the quadrivalent human papillomavirus vaccine in perinatally HIV infected girls
entry.content:
This case study converts a conceptual DAG into a data-specific DAG, exposing residual bias from sexual-history misclassification, proxies for structural racism, and missing maternal history.

The authors

Ruby Barnard-Mayers, Eleanor J Murray, and colleagues are affiliated with Boston University, Harvard, NYU, UCLA, the University of Miami, and the PHACS network. The team spans epidemiology, pediatric HIV, HPV, and health decision science. The DAG is published before analysis with an invitation for community feedback.

The problem

Girls with perinatally acquired HIV appeared to have more abnormal cervical cytology after quadrivalent HPV vaccination. This could reflect immune response or age, vaccination access, sexual behavior, structural factors, and measurement error. The authors aim to specify mechanisms, target population, and data limitations before analysis.

The approach

Two questions concern vaccination versus no vaccination and dose response. Exposure is split into initiation and continuation, with vaccine response as a mediator. Literature and expert discussion add nodes affecting at least two variables. Mixed or limited evidence defaults to retaining an arrow because an omitted edge encodes the stronger sharp-null assumption.

The conceptual DAG is then adapted to PHACS AMP and AMP Up data. Measured sexual history, measured antibody response, race or ethnicity as a proxy, and unavailable maternal cervical history are represented separately. Handling the yeast-allergy contraindication changes when the target population shifts from all girls with perinatal HIV to those eligible for vaccination.

Key results

This is a design case study and estimates no vaccine effect. For the total effect of vaccine initiation on abnormal cytology, the conceptual graph gives an adjustment set of age, insurance, structural racism, maternal history, and sexual history.

After adapting to data, true sexual history can remain a confounder: 14%–39% of children and adolescents with perinatal HIV exposure in an earlier study inconsistently reported lifetime partners or condom use. Structural racism and maternal cervical history are unavailable, requiring imperfect proxies or quantitative bias analysis. Antibody titers are also an imperfect measure of immune function.

How solid is the evidence

Textual rationales accompany key edges, while omitted edges are treated as assumptions requiring defense. Separating measurements from constructs links data limitations to specific residual confounding and measurement bias. Because no effect analysis is performed, the paper does not answer whether the vaccine works or whether the proposed set is sufficient in the actual data.

Some decisions rely on team knowledge, such as treating CD4 count, viral load, and ART as modifiers rather than confounders. Publishing those disagreements makes the DAG explicitly revisable.

entry.title: Evidence synthesis for constructing directed acyclic graphs (ESC-DAGs): a novel and systematic method for building directed acyclic graphs
entry.content:
ESC-DAGs maps study conclusions into saturated graphs, audits candidate edges, and integrates them; it makes graph construction traceable while leaving edge-level evidence quality unresolved.

The authors

Karl D Ferguson, Mark McCann, Srinivasa Vittal Katikireddi, Hilary Thomson, Michael J Green, and James D Lewsey are mainly affiliated with public health, mental health, and health economics groups at the University of Glasgow. Published in the International Journal of Epidemiology, this is the original ESC-DAGs methods paper.

The problem

DAGs can identify adjustment strategies through the backdoor criterion, yet papers seldom explain where their graph came from. Intuitive graph drawing is difficult to audit and can be shaped by available data or results. The authors embed evidence synthesis in the review phase and preserve decisions about nodes, edges, and deletions.

The approach

The workflow has mapping, translation, and integration. Mapping turns each study's exposure, outcome, and controls into an implied graph: exposure points to outcome, controls initially become common causes, and nodes are saturated with possible links. This represents the study's claims without judging correctness.

Translation checks both directions of each candidate edge. Failed temporality deletes or reverses an edge; face validity removes obvious implausibility; theory enters a log but its absence is not proof of no effect; a potential-outcomes thought experiment asks whether setting everyone to alternative exposure values would change the average outcome. Integration adds retained edges to a blank graph, reviews newly possible relations, and may recombine theoretically similar nodes with matching inputs and outputs.

Key results

This is a methods demonstration without effect estimates or sample-size results. A hypothetical and an empirical study of parental and adolescent alcohol use illustrate the process. An initial graph treats adolescent sex and substance use as confounders. Translation shows sex as a separate risk path and substance use as a possible mediator; indiscriminate adjustment can block the effect and open collider bias.

The integrated DAG can be analyzed in DAGitty and low-confidence or bidirectional candidate edges can define structural sensitivity analyses. The paper recommends publishing decision logs and graph code.

How solid is the evidence

The contribution is transparency, systematization, and modularity, not empirical validation. Saturated mapping provisionally treats every adjusted variable as a common cause, then relies heavily on reviewers to correct it. A biased regression conclusion is still inherited at the mapping stage.

Temporality, face validity, theory, and counterfactual questions do not become reproducible evidence weights. An edge retained from any study can enter the integrated graph without formal weighting by design, risk of bias, or independence. True structures may still be missed, and the workload is substantial.

entry.title: How to use directed acyclic graphs: guide for clinical researchers
entry.content:
This seven-step guide connects estimands, consensus DAGs, data choice, statistical analysis, and bias sensitivity analysis into one clinical-research workflow.

The authors

Timothy Feeney is affiliated with the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill; Fernando Pires Hartwig with the Federal University of Pelotas and the University of Bristol; Neil M Davies with University College London, Bristol, and the Norwegian University of Science and Technology. The authors work in epidemiology and causal-inference methods.

The problem

DAG use in clinical research is increasing, yet a review of 234 articles found incomplete reporting of assumptions and adjustment sets. This article seeks to turn DAGs from publication graphics into traceable design tools that can change data choice, analysis, and interpretation.

The approach

Using heavy alcohol use and five-year mortality, the authors propose seven steps: define population, exposure, outcome, and estimand; identify variables through literature and experts; iterate to consensus and preregister; choose data and measurement from the graph; choose analysis; design sensitivity analyses for missing or mismeasured variables; and publish the DAG, code, and deviations.

The object for adjustment is the set of backdoor paths in the consensus graph. A variable set is triggered when it blocks all confounding paths without blocking the target causal path; the practical effect is specification of primary covariates. Missing key variables trigger new data, a different dataset, or quantitative bias analysis rather than an automatic unbiased estimate.

Key results

This is a methods guide, with no new clinical effect estimate. The alcohol example yields two minimal sufficient sets: socioeconomic position, care access, and heavy alcohol use; or care access, heavy alcohol use, and family history of alcohol abuse. Adding potentially unmeasured family support can eliminate any sufficient set composed only of measured variables.

Steps 4–6 connect the graph to data: identify required measurements, disclose gaps, and translate unmeasured confounding and measurement error into sensitivity-analysis parameters. Step 7 calls for publication of the graph and reproducible code.

How solid is the evidence

The recommendations rest on DAG theory, applied reviews, and author experience. They are appropriate as reporting guidance, but no comparison shows that the seven-step workflow reduces bias. Expert-consensus thresholds and edge-level evidence grades are unspecified. A carefully executed workflow can still produce a wrong consensus graph.

The article emphasizes that omitted nodes and arrows are strong assumptions. DAGs do not quantify bias magnitude, infer direction from data alone, or automatically solve changing adherence, time-varying confounding, and inconsistent intervention definitions.

entry.title: Evaluating Directed Acyclic Graphs with DAGMetrics: Insights from Tuber and Soil Microbiome Data
entry.content:
DAGMetrics compares complexity and stability among learned DAGs; without a ground-truth graph, these metrics select structures but cannot confirm causal direction.

The authors

Pavel Averin and Theodoros Moysiadis are affiliated with the University of Nicosia; Ifigeneia Mellidou and Aliki Xanthopoulou with Greece's ELGO-DIMITRA Institute of Plant Breeding and Genetic Resources; Maria Ganopoulou with Aristotle University of Thessaloniki. The team spans computer science, plant research, and microbiome analysis.

The problem

Causal-discovery outputs can change with tests, scores, scaling, and transformations. Real microbiome datasets rarely have a known DAG, leaving researchers unsure which graph merits interpretation. DAGMetrics provides one interface for global and local structural comparisons.

The approach

The R package reports edges, colliders, and isolated nodes, compares graphs using F1 and shared-edge counts, and visualizes Markov blankets. On potato tuber and soil microbiomes, the authors compare PC, Inter-IAMB, Tabu Search, and Hill-Climbing with Kendall, Spearman, or Pearson tests, AIC or BIC scores, three scaling options, and two transformations.

The analysis produces 240 tuber DAGs and 120 soil DAGs. The selection object is graph structure across configurations. Moderate complexity and stability across preprocessing trigger selection; the practical effect is choosing candidate graphs for Markov-blanket and biological interpretation.

Key results

Score-based methods generally produce denser graphs, while constraint-based methods produce more isolated nodes. PC and Inter-IAMB with Spearman or Kendall are relatively stable across scaling and transformations. PC with Spearman is favored as a compromise between complexity and stability.

Tuber Markov blankets contain up to seven nodes and soil blankets up to four, with many isolated nodes. Some edges recur across terroirs and storage stages and align with known microbial ecology. Outputs from different algorithm families remain highly dissimilar.

How solid is the evidence

Two real datasets and 360 graphs demonstrate software function and structural sensitivity. There is no ground-truth DAG, so F1 measures agreement between estimated graphs, not accuracy. A stable error scores well, and shared assumptions can make algorithms jointly miss latent variables, compositional constraints, or batch effects.

Microbial abundance data are high-dimensional, small-sample, and compositional, which can distort standard conditional-independence tests. Biological interpretation is largely post hoc, without intervention or independent-cohort validation.

entry.title: CLADDER: Assessing Causal Reasoning in Language Models
entry.content:
CLADDER turns natural-language questions into tasks with answers determined by a causal inference engine; GPT-4 reaches only 70.40% even with a bespoke reasoning chain.

The authors

Zhijing Jin, Yuen Chen, Felix Leeb, Luigi Gresele, and colleagues are affiliated with the Max Planck Institute for Intelligent Systems, ETH Zürich, IIT Kharagpur, the University of Hong Kong, and the University of Washington. The team combines NLP, causal inference, and cognitive science, with supervision from Bernhard Schölkopf and Mrinmaya Sachan.

The problem

Claims that a model “understands causality” often rely on commonsense questions or causal-phrase extraction, which can be solved by language patterns. CLADDER tests whether models parse a graph, classify a query, invoke the right identification rule, and calculate an answer while limiting contamination and commonsense shortcuts.

The approach

Binary and categorical causal graphs generate questions across association, intervention, and counterfactual rungs. An inference engine derives symbolic questions and ground-truth probabilities before templates verbalize them. Contexts are commonsensical, nonsensical, or anti-commonsensical.

CausalCoT uses six stages: parse the graph, identify the query, formalize it, collect data, perform causal inference, and calculate the binary answer. Evaluation covers final accuracy and intermediate node, edge, query or estimand, inference, and arithmetic performance.

Key results

CLADDER v1.5 contains 10,112 samples: 3,160, 3,160, and 3,792 across rungs 1, 2, and 3. Random accuracy is 49.27%; LLaMa scores 44.03%, Alpaca 44.66%, GPT-3.5 52.18%, and GPT-4 62.03%.

CausalCoT raises GPT-4 to 70.40%, an 8.37-point gain. Rung accuracies are 83.35%, 67.47%, and 62.05%. Anti-commonsense accuracy rises from 60.47% to 70.12%. Node and edge F1 are 99.34% and 97.01%, yet query or estimand F1 is 53% and formal-inference F1 is 50.65%.

How solid is the evidence

Ground truth comes from explicit models and an inference engine. Nonsensical and anti-commonsense contexts reduce memorization shortcuts, and stepwise evaluation localizes failures. These remain closed-world synthetic tasks: the graph, variables, and identifiability are supplied. Target populations, temporal ordering, unmeasured confounding, and measurement mechanisms are outside the benchmark.

The models and APIs are a 2023 snapshot and do not determine current-model performance. Some fine-grained evaluations use only 2,000 questions because of rate limits, and accuracy treats all errors as equally costly.

entry.title: Directed acyclic graph helps to understand the causality of malnutrition in under-5 children born small for gestational age
entry.content:
A 21-node DAG synthesized from 342 abstracts yields 12 adjustment options; it can guide a future study but does not establish the magnitude of the SGA effect on malnutrition.

The authors

Soumya Tiwari, Viswas Chhapola, Nisha Chaudhary, and Lokesh Sharma are pediatricians at Lady Hardinge Medical College in New Delhi. The clinical team applies ESC-DAGs-style causal criteria to a South Asian child-nutrition question.

The problem

Term small-for-gestational-age status is associated with under-five malnutrition, while fetal growth restriction, poverty, feeding, and maternal health are connected. Regression that adjusts for everything available can condition on mediators or colliders. The authors first define effects relevant to different stakeholders, then build a graph and adjustment sets for future research.

The approach

Exposure is term SGA and outcome is WHO-defined malnutrition from six months to five years. Estimands are framed for policymakers, clinicians, and parents. PubMed, Scopus, Web of Science, and ProQuest were searched through 15 December 2023, restricted to the previous ten years and the Indian subcontinent.

The rapid narrative review extracts factors from abstracts. Candidate directions pass through temporality, face validity, recourse to theory, and counterfactual thought experiments before entry in DAGitty. The object of adjustment is every backdoor path from SGA to malnutrition. A set is triggered when it blocks all such paths without post-exposure variables; its practical effect is the minimum allowable covariate combination for regression.

Key results

The search found 4,818 records and retained 342 abstracts; 39% of studies were from India and 27% from Bangladesh. Eighty-one factors were grouped into 17 nodes across socioeconomic, parental, child-related, environmental, and political domains.

The final DAG contains 21 nodes. DAGitty identifies 12 minimal sufficient adjustment sets, one containing nine variables. No original effects are pooled and no cohort data estimate the actual effect of term SGA on malnutrition.

How solid is the evidence

Defining the question before using explicit edge criteria is more transparent than P-value-based selection. Evidence extraction remains abstract-only, preventing checks of definitions, timing, adjustment, and risk of bias. The ten-year and regional restrictions can omit common causes, while grouping 81 factors into 17 nodes hides measurement and temporal differences.

The 12 sets are sufficient only under the assumed graph. Similar estimates across them would increase structural compatibility, not validate the graph. There is no expert consensus process, external audit, or data-based sensitivity analysis.

entry.title: How to develop causal directed acyclic graphs for observational health research: a scoping review
entry.content:
Existing DAG guidance broadly agrees on drawing mechanics but rarely explains where domain knowledge comes from or why an edge exists; this scoping review adds selection and measurement processes to a six-step workflow.

The authors

Louise Poppe, Johan Steen, Wen Wei Loh, Peter W. G. Tennant, and colleagues are affiliated with Ghent University, Maastricht University, Emory University, the University of Leeds, the Alan Turing Institute, and other institutions. The team spans health psychology, clinical medicine, statistics, and causal inference.

The problem

DAGs guide covariate selection, yet they are often drawn shortly before analysis with opaque provenance for nodes and edges. The authors review development guidance, compare treatments of confounding, selection, measurement error, and domain knowledge, and propose a minimum practical workflow.

The approach

The scoping review identified 902 unique database records and also considered web and book resources, retaining 11 guidance sources. Two reviewers screened records. Development purpose, steps, knowledge sources, and reporting recommendations were charted. A running example examines physical-activity guidelines and working memory in older adults.

The six steps are: create the DAG early; define exposure and outcome precisely; add common causes; consider selection nodes; consider measurement-error nodes; and use the DAG in analysis. The object for common-cause assessment is an open noncausal exposure–outcome path. A common cause triggers adjustment-set inclusion; selection and measurement nodes reveal biases that ordinary covariate adjustment may not repair.

Key results

Most sources use DAGs to guide analysis, but disagree between sequentially adding confounders and removing edges from a saturated graph. Few discuss selection nodes, only one directly covers measurement-error nodes, and only one source explains how to search the literature for domain knowledge.

The authors therefore make selection and measurement explicit steps and recommend documenting knowledge acquisition and graph decisions. Their example shows how volunteering creates a collider and why questionnaire-measured activity and test-measured working memory should be separated from their underlying constructs.

How solid is the evidence

This is a scoping review of guidance, designed to describe and synthesize recommendations. It does not test which workflow recovers a graph closer to causal truth. Only 11 resources were included, grey literature cannot be exhaustive, and the six steps reflect synthesis rather than prospective validation.

Graph validity still depends on domain knowledge. Missing a common-cause edge can leave residual confounding; adding an unnecessary edge often enlarges the adjustment set; conditioning on a collider or mediator can create bias. Standard DAGs also do not solve treatment-confounder feedback, where ordinary regression may fail.

entry.title: Conducting umbrella reviews
entry.content:
A sound umbrella review places multiple meta-analyses under one re-analysis and grading protocol; it provides a landscape and priorities but cannot repair shared upstream bias.

The authors

Lazaros Belbasis, Vanesa Bellou, and John P A Ioannidis have extensive experience in meta-research and evidence credibility. Their affiliations span meta-research groups in Berlin, Ioannina, and Stanford. This is a BMJ Medicine research methods and reporting article.

The problem

When many systematic reviews already exist for one disease, another single-factor review cannot answer which interventions or risk factors have the strongest evidence. Umbrella reviews compare many questions, but overlapping reviews, inconsistent statistics, and secondary-data errors can compound bias. This article defines when and how to conduct one.

The approach

Four stages anchor the workflow: search and selection, data extraction, standardized re-analysis and grading, and interpretation. Eligibility specifies population, intervention or exposure, comparator, and outcome; two researchers screen and extract independently. Overlapping reviews may be prioritized by recency, study count, prospective evidence, or quality, with exclusions reported.

Investigators should extract study-specific effects, 95% CIs, sample sizes, and event counts, then rerun every meta-analysis under one protocol. GRADE can address interventions. Epidemiological associations are judged by evidence volume, P value, heterogeneity, 95% prediction interval, small-study effects, and excess significance. Confounding, reverse causation, selection, and information bias are interpreted separately.

Key results

This is a methods guide, not a new clinical-effect study. Its working example is an umbrella review of non-genetic type 2 diabetes risk factors: 86 articles and 142 associations were included. Although 116 associations had P<0.05, only 11 associations met strong-evidence criteria combining evidence amount, significance, prediction intervals, heterogeneity, and bias signals.

The authors also cite an empirical assessment of 57 umbrella reviews and 3,744 observational meta-analyses, where the grading features contributed largely independent information. Box 3 specifies the required reporting set, including cases, sample size, model, pooled estimate, prediction interval, largest study, and bias tests.

How solid is the evidence

Methodological experience, empirical meta-research, and a worked example support process guidance, not any specific medical relationship. Umbrella reviews cover only topics already synthesized. Bias can accumulate across primary studies, meta-analyses, and the umbrella review itself, while complete verification against every original report is rarely feasible.

The object of grading is each meta-analysis; prespecified statistical and bias criteria trigger a tier; the practical effect is prioritization for further validation. Even the top tier needs design and biological assessment before a causal claim.

entry.title: Risk factors for preterm birth: an umbrella review of meta-analyses of observational studies
entry.content:
Only seven of 166 preterm-birth factors meet prespecified epidemiological credibility criteria; the grade filters noise but remains distinct from causal evidence.

The authors

Ioannis Mitrogiannis, Stefania Papatheodorou, and colleagues bring together obstetrics, epidemiology, and evidence synthesis. The review was registered in PROSPERO (CRD42021227296) and re-analyzed observational meta-analyses under one protocol.

The problem

The literature on preterm-birth risk factors is large, while significant findings are vulnerable to heterogeneity, small-study effects, and selective reporting. The authors seek a broad map and identify associations meeting joint requirements for sample size, statistical strength, prediction intervals, and bias diagnostics.

The approach

PubMed and Scopus were searched through February 2021 for systematic reviews and meta-analyses of observational studies. For overlapping reviews of the same factor, the most comprehensive source was retained. Each association was re-estimated with random effects, 95% CI, 95% prediction interval, I², small-study effects, and excess significance.

The object graded as robust evidence is one risk-factor meta-analysis. It is triggered by more than 1,000 cases, P<10⁻⁶, I²<50%, a prediction interval excluding the null, and no small-study or excess-significance signal. The practical effect is placement in the highest epidemiological credibility tier. The rule does not test unmeasured confounding or whether the exposure is intervenable.

Key results

The review analyzes 85 meta-analyses, 1,480 primary studies, and 166 associations. Ninety-nine associations are significant at P<0.05 and 41 at P<10⁻⁶. Ninety-one have large or very large heterogeneity; 37 show small-study effects and 12 show excess significance.

Seven meet robust criteria: amphetamine exposure, isolated single umbilical artery, maternal personality disorder, objectively assessed sleep-disordered breathing, prior induced termination with vacuum aspiration, low gestational weight gain, and an interpregnancy interval under six months after miscarriage. Twenty-six are highly suggestive and 15 suggestive.

How solid is the evidence

Standardized re-analysis and prespecified tiers improve comparability, and prediction intervals are stricter than pooled P values alone. All seven robust findings arise from meta-analyses with fewer than ten studies, where bias tests have limited power. The authors did not reassess each primary study, and omissions from source reviews carry forward.

“Robust” describes statistical and publication patterns. It does not remove confounding, reverse causation, selection bias, or exposure misclassification. Some factors are compound social or clinical states rather than direct interventions.

entry.title: Causal knowledge graph construction and evaluation for clinical decision support of diabetic nephropathy
entry.content:
This study links causal statements from literature, guidelines, and textbooks to patient-level graph reasoning; it demonstrates engineering utility, not counterfactual validity of the edges.

The authors

Kewei Lyu, Yu Tian, and corresponding author Jingsong Li are affiliated with Zhejiang University and the Research Center for Healthcare Data Science at Zhejiang Lab. Xi Yao, Ping Zhang, and Jianghua Chen are based at the Kidney Disease Center of the First Affiliated Hospital, Zhejiang University. The collaboration spans knowledge engineering, electronic records, and nephrology.

The problem

Medical causal knowledge graphs are often justified by extraction scale. Two practical questions receive less attention: whether a graph covers diagnostic evidence in real records, and whether noisy edges degrade clinical decision support. The authors use differential diagnosis of diabetic versus non-diabetic kidney disease to assess completeness, predictive use, and accuracy after pruning.

The approach

The graph combines SemMedDB, UpToDate, and Churchill's Pocketbook of Differential Diagnosis. Causes, produces, and predisposes relations are normalized, with PMID or source provenance retained. Structured, semi-structured, and narrative EHR evidence is mapped into patient-specific graphs. Two pruning rules remove SemMedDB edges using literature co-occurrence and causal-relation ratios.

Evaluation uses 1,427 inpatients and 3,045 admissions from one Chinese hospital between October 2010 and October 2020. Patient-level training, validation, and test sets contain 856, 286, and 285 people. R-GCN, MHGRN, and MedPath compare knowledge sources, causal versus associative relations, and pruning thresholds; 150 patient narratives and expert samples assess coverage and accuracy.

Key results

The graph contains 153,289 concepts and 1,719,968 causal triples, 99.1% from SemMedDB. Mapping was nearly 100% for structured data and 93.5% for semi-structured data. Among 1,783 diagnostic evidence items in 150 patients, causal paths covered 93.6%; the abstract reports overall causal-relation coverage of 93.9%.

Combining all three sources improved AUC and F1 over SemMedDB alone for all three models (P<0.01). Models using only the three causal relations did not differ significantly from models using all nine relations, whereas models excluding them performed worse. Pruning improved AUC and F1 (P<0.01) and raised expert-rated triple accuracy by 5.5 percentage points.

How solid is the evidence

Real EHRs, model ablations, and expert review make this stronger than a graph-size demonstration. The outcomes remain diagnostic prediction and text coverage. An edge helping a model, or appearing plausible to an expert, does not show that intervening on its cause would change the outcome. The graph also inherits extraction and publication biases from SemMedDB, which supplies 99.1% of its triples.

The cohort is single-center and dominated by older men. Threshold selection in the same application can overfit pruning to that task. Edges are not graded by study design, target population, estimand, or risk of bias.

entry.title: Causal Evidence Extraction and Triangulation in Crisis Reports using Large Language Models: A ReliefWeb-based Study
entry.content:
The paper adds query conditioning, evidence snippets, and context stratification to crisis-report extraction. Extraction is strong; its LoE score measures directional concentration across contexts rather than cross-design causal strength.

The authors

Yuanjun Zhang lists the University of Oulu and LUT University as affiliations, while corresponding author Mourad Oussalah is at the University of Oulu. The work was supported by the EU Horizon 2020 and CHANSE Digital Emergency Communication project, DIGeMERGE; the group has also published on explainable multimodal disaster-severity assessment.

The problem

ReliefWeb reports are long and multi-topic. Recall-oriented keyword search retrieves documents in which cash assistance may appear only in passing, and an unconstrained LLM can turn peripheral text into intervention–outcome relations. The paper aims to produce structured records containing an intervention, outcome, direction, strength, and supporting passage, then summarize directional agreement across disaster and source contexts.

The approach

The corpus contains English full-text ReliefWeb reports from 2000–2024 retrieved with “cash assistance.” The authors retain four disaster types—flood, drought, epidemic, and earthquake—and five source groups: academic, government, media, NGO, and Red Cross/Red Crescent. This leaves 8,029 reports. Domain experts annotate 220 relations in a random test sample of 100 reports.

Extraction has two stages. Stage 1 computes intervention–outcome candidates within the query-specified intervention class and attaches supporting snippets; the query condition is intended to suppress retrieval-induced over-extraction. Stage 2 classifies each candidate as increase, decrease, or no clear change and assigns weak, moderate, or strong evidence using the snippet and full report. Intervention and outcome matches use BERTScore≥0.8. Strength is scored only when direction is correct, with 0.5 credit for adjacent-category errors.

Before triangulation, polarity normalization maps positively and negatively framed outcomes such as food security and food insecurity onto a common improvement direction. Each record enters a disaster×source cell. Weak, moderate, and strong receive weights 0, 1, and 2; directional counts receive Laplace smoothing with α=0.1. Non-empty cells are averaged equally. The largest of the three global directional probabilities is linearly rescaled from 1/3–1 into LoE 0–1. Temporal trajectories repeat the calculation cumulatively by year.

The best proprietary pipeline also generates training labels for two Llama-3.1-8B LoRA adapters, one per stage. The 100 expert-annotated reports are excluded from distillation.

Key results

Two-step Qwen-Plus with query conditioning and snippet grounding reaches weighted F1 90.73%. Llama-3.1-8B rises from 75.12% without fine-tuning to 94.15% after LoRA SFT. The weighted score combines intervention, outcome, direction, and strength at 0.30, 0.30, 0.20, and 0.20.

The selected proprietary pipeline extracts 19,568 records from 8,029 reports. The food-outcome analysis retains 19 non-empty disaster×source cells and reports PP=0.91 and LoE=0.865. The cumulative series peaks in 2017 at PP=0.953 and LoE=0.929 before softening modestly from 2018–2024. Polarity labels cover 941 outcome strings occurring at least three times, representing 13,554 records or 69.27% of all extractions.

How solid is the evidence

The extraction study includes an expert test set, ablations, and explicit scoring rules. Query conditioning improves results consistently across three API models. The test set contains only 100 reports, however, and the paper gives no inter-annotator agreement. A BERTScore threshold may count loosely similar relations as matches. The ACL responsible-research checklist says fixed configurations were run once, without means, standard deviations, or error bars. Teacher-generated distillation labels can pass systematic model errors to the student.

For LoE, the computational object is the largest cell-averaged directional probability. Dominance by one direction triggers a larger score; the practical effect is a 0–1 measure of concentration. The calculation does not align causal estimands or test whether biases are independent across reports, disasters, or sources. Giving weak evidence a weight of zero removes it from directional counts. Equal cell weighting limits domination by high-volume sources but leaves shared reporting bias untouched.

A sentence in a report claiming that cash assistance improved food security is a causal statement in text, not necessarily a controlled effect estimate. English-only accessible text, one retrieval phrase, and restricted disaster and source categories narrow generalizability. The authors appropriately frame the output as decision support requiring human review and state that strength labels describe reported evidence, not effect magnitude or population impact.

entry.title: Enhancing system safety analysis in aviation: A causal knowledge graph framework powered by Large Language Models for proactive risk identification
entry.content:
Extract causal triples from 65,535 aviation narratives, normalize their predicates, and use path pruning for graph QA; the engineering chain is complete, but its edges remain reporters' causal statements.

The authors

Jifei Cheng, Donghui Shi, and Yakun Sun are with the Department of Computer Engineering at Anhui Jianzhu University; Jozef Zurada is with the University of Louisville College of Business. The paper became available online on 13 July 2026 and appears in volume 277 of Reliability Engineering & System Safety, dated 2027.

The problem

Aviation safety reports contain causal narratives linking equipment, human factors, and the environment, but the corpus is large and linguistically inconsistent. LLM extraction creates another problem: variants such as “led to,” “caused,” and “resulted in” fragment equivalent edges.

Standard KG-QA then retrieves a whole three-hop neighborhood, allowing noise to swamp useful paths. The paper connects extraction, relation normalization, and risk-oriented QA in one automated pipeline.

The approach

The source is 65,535 voluntary NASA ASRS reports from January 1988 through July 2012. In stage one, Gemma3-12B receives two examples and extracts open-schema subject-relation-object triples from explicit causal language. Stage two processes 2,939 raw predicates.

MiniLM computes cosine similarity; when similarity reaches 0.5, an LLM reviews whether meaning, direction, and granularity permit a merge. The process repeats until no candidate can be merged. This reduces the vocabulary to 185 relation types while retaining 394,770 triples and 562,020 entities.

For QA, the query is embedded to select seed nodes and extract three-hop subgraphs. A candidate path is retained only when an LLM's relevance score exceeds the pruning threshold; surviving paths are serialized and passed to GPT-4o with a chain-of-thought prompt. The operational effect is less neighborhood noise, with the corresponding risk that secondary paths receive low scores and disappear.

Key results

On a 1,000-report extraction set, two-shot GPT-4o reached F1 0.8302 and Gemma3-12B 0.7986. At the 0.5 normalization threshold, MiniLM alone reached F1 0.749; LLM review raised it to 0.908. Seven iterations reduced 2,939 predicates to 185, a 93.71% decrease.

On 100 QA pairs, the full model scored 0.9682 for answer relevance, 0.9310 for semantic similarity, 0.6583 for context precision, and 0.5923 for context recall. The last two metrics were 0.0844 and 0.1222 for Vanilla KG-QA. Four supplementary human checks produced Cohen's kappa values from 0.7093 to 0.7357.

How solid is the evidence

The evaluation covers extraction, normalization, and QA, and the ablation holds the graph and answer generator constant. The normalization success criterion is higher F1 plus human agreement; that supports synonym consolidation, not causal identification. The reference triples combine GPT-3.5 output with manual annotation, and unmatched outputs are reconsidered by an LLM and NLI model.

LLMs participate in both normalized-label generation and scoring, creating circular evaluation that the authors acknowledge. ASRS contains voluntary retrospective narratives, without unreported events or an exposure denominator such as flight hours. Edge frequency therefore mixes mechanism, reporting behavior, and extraction error.

The graph has no target estimand, temporal ordering, intervention definition, or identification assumptions. Its paths and centrality cannot support the paper's “what-if” or intervention-effect language. Data end in 2012, and there is no external-corpus or operational-decision validation.

entry.title: Roadmap for systematic identification and analysis of multiple biases in causal inference
entry.content:
Use an ideal trial and a DAG to expose assumptions first, then put selection, confounding, and measurement bias into one quantitative model; separate sensitivity analyses can misstate their joint impact.

The authors

The work is led by Rushani Wijesuriya and Margarita Moreno-Betancur's Clinical Epidemiology & Biostatistics Unit at the Murdoch Children's Research Institute, with collaborators at the University of Melbourne, the University of Bristol's MRC Integrative Epidemiology Unit, and the University of Queensland.

It was published online in the American Journal of Epidemiology on 16 July 2026 and is currently an accepted manuscript.

The problem

Observational causal analyses simultaneously depend on exchangeability, consistency, positivity, and measurement assumptions. Quantitative bias analyses often choose one violation to quantify while leaving the rest embedded in the primary analysis. Even a series of separate analyses assumes, each time, that the other biases are absent.

That is a poor guide to the combined effect when biases are dependent or point in opposite directions.

The approach

The roadmap has three steps. First, define the ideal randomized trial through its eligibility criteria, treatment strategies, assignment, follow-up, and outcome measurement. For each component, compare the observational emulation with the ideal and state the identification assumptions required to bridge the gap, drawing them in a primary-analysis DAG where possible.

Second, add substantively plausible violations to an alternative DAG. The HealthNuts example asks for the causal effect of any breastfeeding in the first 12 months on asthma risk at age six among 5,279 infants.

Its alternative graph adds generalizability bias from restricting participation by English ability and consent, collider selection from outcome loss to follow-up, unmeasured confounding by gestational hypertension, and exposure/outcome error from parental reports.

Third, target the ideal joint distribution containing the true exposure and outcome, an unmeasured confounder, and the selection process. When the alternative graph implies dependence among these variables, the trigger for joint analysis is met.

The authors factor a bias-adjusting probability according to the graph, specify logistic bias models, sample the true exposure, outcome, and unmeasured confounder, derive inverse-probability selection weights, and fit a weighted outcome regression.

Each Monte Carlo draw yields one effect estimate adjusted for all represented biases. Distributions over bias parameters and bootstrap resampling propagate parameter uncertainty and sampling error into the interval.

Key results

The case study used 1,000 probabilistic draws and 1,000 bootstraps. The jointly adjusted breastfeeding risk difference was about -0.10 versus roughly -0.05 in the primary analysis; the intervals overlapped considerably, so the authors describe only a somewhat stronger protective association. The proof-of-concept simulation is sharper: 1,000 datasets of n=2,000 with a true risk ratio of 0.51.

Simultaneous analysis produced a mean log risk ratio of -0.70, 5.3% relative bias, and 0.92 coverage. The primary analysis averaged -0.32 with 52.8% relative bias. Every one-at-a-time analysis had more than 20% relative bias; outcome-measurement correction alone even reversed the estimate to 2.60 on the log scale.

How solid is the evidence

The paper completes the whole chain from assumption inventory through an alternative DAG to joint analysis, and releases its code. Its simulation criterion is concrete: compare estimates with the known true log risk ratio; below 10% relative bias the joint method is approximately unbiased, while above 20% the one-at-a-time methods materially miss the target.

The result is conditional on a correct alternative DAG, correct bias models, and defensible parameter distributions.

Those parameters are not identified by the primary data; some conditional parameters are approximated with marginal literature values and others rely on expert judgment. The simulation covers one simplified graph with binary variables. DAGs also cannot display every form of measurement or generalizability bias.

Joint and separate estimates barely differed in the applied example, so the advantage still needs testing under more structures, model misspecification, and independent real-data applications. The accepted manuscript may change during copyediting or typesetting.

entry.title: Multiomics insight into disease trajectories of cardiometabolic diseases and cancer
entry.content:
Tracking how cardiometabolic disease and cancer stack into multimorbidity and then death across 429,000 UK Biobank participants: multimorbidity costs about five years of life, which disease comes first shifts mortality risk, and proteomics reads the trajectory earlier than genomics.

The authors

Victor W. Zhong's group in the Department of Epidemiology and Biostatistics, School of Public Health, Shanghai Jiao Tong University School of Medicine, with Xuanwei Jiang and Guangrui Yang as co–first authors and co-corresponding roles for Xihao Du (SJTU) and Chunlai Zeng (Lishui).

Zhong, previously at Northwestern, works on cardiometabolic and nutritional epidemiology and has a run of UK Biobank multimorbidity studies; this continues that line. Published in Nature Communications.

The problem

Multimorbidity is a defining problem of aging populations: roughly a third of people aged 45–65 live with two or more chronic diseases, over 80% among those 80+. Yet most research manages one disease at a time, leaving no map of how multiple chronic diseases evolve into and off each other over time.

Cardiometabolic disease (CMD — cardiovascular disease plus type 2 diabetes) and cancer are two leading causes of death; they're known to co-occur, share risk factors like aging and obesity, and share pathways like inflammation and hypoxia — but who comes first, how much higher the mortality of multimorbidity is over single disease, and whether temporal order changes outcomes were unresolved.

A second gap is prediction: single-disease omics prediction already reaches 10–15 years ahead, but for high-multimorbidity-risk elders, predicting one disease isn't enough, and whether multiomics can capture the transitions between diseases was untested.

The approach

They cast each person's course as a multistate model: five states — healthy, CMD only, cancer only, CMD-cancer multimorbidity, death — linked by nine transitions (e.g., healthy→CMD, CMD→cancer, CMD→cancer→death, cancer→CMD→death).

Across 429,555 UK Biobank participants over a median 14.5 years, they estimated transition probabilities, mean survival times, and mortality risk, deliberately separating "CMD before cancer" from "cancer before CMD." To make states comparable, they used propensity score matching to balance baseline.

The second part is multiomics: in genomics (375,239), metabolomics (234,452), and proteomics (44,816) datasets, they built omics scores (GenScore/MetScore/ProtScore) with LASSO in a discovery set, validated in replication, and measured how much C-statistic each adds over a base model (demographics etc.) for every transition.

Subgroups covered CMD subtype (stroke, heart failure, coronary disease, T2D), cancer survival tier, benign vs. malignant, and CMD severity.

Key results

Over follow-up, 105,903 progressed to single morbidity and 15,088 to CMD-cancer multimorbidity. Multimorbidity carried a 28–33% higher death probability than healthy (5.0–5.3 fewer years of life) and 11–23% higher than single morbidity (2.4–3.6 fewer years); progression to multimorbidity rose sharply about 5 years after either first diagnosis.

Mean survival times: healthy 14.8 years, CMD only 13.2, cancer only 12.3, multimorbidity ~9.5–9.9.

Order matters but flips by cancer type: overall, CMD-first was 1–2% more lethal than cancer-first (0.3 more years lost), but for high-survival cancers CMD-first was 7% lower, for low-survival cancers 9% higher, and 3% higher in the malignant subgroup.

Cancer type dominated — pancreatic cancer had 72% direct mortality; 25% of liver-cancer patients further developed CMD. Among omics, proteomics scores predicted best, with ΔC-statistic 0.03–0.14 across the nine transitions, clearly beating genomics and metabolomics scores (modest at best).

How solid is the evidence

The strengths are scale and design: 429,555 people, a median 14.5 years of follow-up, and a multistate framework that puts progression, order, and death into one coherent structure, with propensity matching improving comparability and the proteomics gain validated in replication. But several things need care.

A multistate model yields transition probabilities and associations, not intervention effects — "CMD-first is more lethal" is an observed temporal association still open to shared causes, detection timing, and competing risk.

Propensity matching only balances measured baseline; aging, obesity, and inflammation, which drive both disease families, are hard to balance, so unmeasured confounding persists. The 44,816 with proteomics are the Olink subset of the cohort, so representativeness and external validity are open questions the authors themselves flag.

The ΔC-statistic of 0.03–0.14 is real but small, and proteomics' "win" is mostly relative to genomics/metabolomics — absolute discrimination is short of clinical use.

UK Biobank's healthy-volunteer bias and largely white sample limit extrapolation.

entry.title: Comparative effects of drugs for adults with overweight or obesity: systematic review and network meta-analysis
entry.content:
Pooling 262 obesity-drug trials into one network, tirzepatide and CagriSema deliver the most weight loss at a year (~15%), but bigger losses come with more harms and discontinuation, most drugs don't improve quality of life, and only subcutaneous semaglutide shows cardiovascular and mortality benefit.

The authors

Led by Sheyu Li (Department of Endocrinology and the Chinese Evidence-based Medicine Center, West China Hospital, Sichuan University), with Kailei Nong among the co–first authors, and a heavyweight methods roster: GRADE co-founder Gordon Guyatt (McMaster), Per Olav Vandvik and Thomas Agoritsas of the MAGIC Evidence Ecosystem Foundation, diabetes epidemiologist Kamlesh Khunti, and obesity specialist Carel Le Roux.

It's part of the BMJ Rapid Recommendations (a BMJ–MAGIC collaboration), with results meant to feed a global guideline panel, following the same living-network-meta-analysis model they used for type 2 diabetes drugs. Published in BMJ.

The problem

Obesity drugs have proliferated — GLP-1s, GIP/GLP-1 dual agonists, triple agonists, plus older agents like phentermine-topiramate and naltrexone-bupropion.

Clinicians, payers, and patients must choose among a dozen-plus drugs, but prior network meta-analyses mostly covered only approved drugs and only weight loss, without putting the newer agents, the overall benefit-harm balance, and the certainty of evidence transparently side by side.

What was missing was a landscape covering all available obesity drugs across patient-important outcomes beyond weight (cardiovascular, mortality, quality of life, body composition, adverse events), each tagged with GRADE certainty, to support genuinely informed decisions.

The approach

A systematic review and network meta-analysis, PRISMA-NMA reported and PROSPERO registered, done as a BMJ Rapid Recommendation. They searched Medline, Embase, and Cochrane through November 2025, including RCTs of ≥12 weeks comparing an obesity drug against lifestyle modification, placebo, or another drug, in adults with overweight or obesity (with or without cardiovascular/metabolic complications).

The value of a network meta-analysis is that it compares many interventions at once, including pairs never tested head-to-head (via indirect comparison), provided the network's transitivity/consistency holds. Synthesis used frequentist random-effects models for the main results plus Bayesian dose-response models, with heterogeneity and inconsistency checked.

Risk of bias used Cochrane RoB 2, and certainty was GRADE-rated for each of 24 outcomes.

Subgroups by dose, duration, and key patient characteristics, plus sensitivity analyses, were done. The point of the whole exercise isn't a ranking but a base for a guideline panel to move from evidence to risk-stratified recommendations.

Key results

262 trials, 99,791 participants, 19 drugs, follow-up 12–172 weeks. Weight loss at one year vs lifestyle alone (moderate-to-high certainty): tirzepatide -14.9% (95% CI -16.0% to -13.9%), CagriSema -14.8%, oral semaglutide -10.9%, orforglipron -9.9%, subcutaneous semaglutide -9.8%, phentermine-topiramate -8.1%; investigational agents (retatrutide, mazdutide) may reach 13–14.6% but at very low certainty.

The cost is symmetric: discontinuation due to adverse events was highest with orforglipron, naltrexone-bupropion, liraglutide, phentermine-topiramate, CagriSema, and oral semaglutide (RR 1.9–4.2); gastrointestinal events and fatigue rose too, with naltrexone-bupropion's fatigue RR at 8.9 (331 more per 1,000 over a year). On body composition, tirzepatide cut fat mass the most (25.7%) but also lean mass the most (8.3%).

On hard outcomes, only subcutaneous semaglutide was tied to lower all-cause mortality (RR 0.81, 0.72–0.93) and myocardial infarction (0.72), largely driven by cardiovascular outcome trials in high-risk groups; subcutaneous semaglutide and tirzepatide lowered heart failure risk.

No drug convincingly reduced kidney failure or improved quality of life beyond the minimally important difference (43 trials, 45,663 participants; all improvements <5 points, MID of 10).

How solid is the evidence

This is the most rigorous tier of this literature: an RCT network, item-by-item RoB2, GRADE on each of 24 outcomes, a separate dose-response model, and an explicit plan for a guideline panel to move from evidence to recommendation.

The thing to watch is the network meta-analysis's Achilles heel, transitivity: ranking drugs never compared head-to-head assumes trial populations, comparators, and outcome definitions are comparable enough; if the newer-drug trials differ systematically in population or placebo response from the older ones, indirect comparisons drift — not a paranoid worry for obesity drugs, where early-agent and GLP-1-era trial populations differ.

Second, certainty varies by outcome: weight loss is moderate-to-high, but hard outcomes and quality of life are often low certainty with short follow-up, so "no benefit" is often "not measurable." Third, the mortality/MI benefit rests almost entirely on a few high-risk CVOTs and should be discounted when extrapolating to general weight-management populations.

GRADE-tagging these certainty differences one by one is exactly what makes it more credible than a bare ranking.

entry.title: Risk for Cancer With Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 Receptor Agonists and Dual Agonists: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis
entry.content:
Pooling 48 placebo-controlled RCTs, GLP-1RAs probably neither raise nor lower obesity-related cancer risk — the odds ratios for thyroid, pancreatic, breast, and kidney cancer all cross 1, and the protective signals seen in observational studies don't hold up under randomized evidence.

The authors

A multicenter, physician-led team; the corresponding and senior author is Cho-Han Chiang (Department of Medicine, Mount Auburn Hospital, Harvard Medical School), with Albert Ko, Yu-Cheng Chang, and Furkan Bahar as co–first authors.

Co-authors are spread across Mount Auburn/Harvard, the University at Buffalo, Albert Einstein/Montefiore, the University of Vermont, and National Taiwan University Hospital and Taipei Tzu Chi, among others — many are Taiwanese-American clinical researchers.

Cross-institution systematic reviews and meta-analyses led by residents/fellows are a familiar output for this kind of group. The review was registered on PROSPERO and published in Annals of Internal Medicine.

The problem

GLP-1RAs and GIP/GLP-1 dual agonists are exploding in use, and the most unsettled safety question is cancer.

The worry comes from both directions: on one side, rodent studies show C-cell hyperplasia and medullary thyroid carcinoma with GLP-1RAs, and insurance-claims data flagged higher thyroid cancer in the first year of use; on the other, several population studies report lower incidence of multiple obesity-related cancers among users, read as a weight-loss cancer dividend.

Both directions rest mostly on observational data, conflict with each other, and can be muddied by study design, exposure duration, baseline cancer risk, and confounders like obesity and smoking. What was missing was a synthesis that isolates the randomized evidence and grades certainty cancer by cancer.

The approach

A systematic review and meta-analysis, PRISMA-reported and PROSPERO-registered.

They searched PubMed, Embase, Web of Science, Scopus, and Cochrane through August 2025, including only placebo-controlled RCTs of FDA-approved agents (semaglutide, dulaglutide, exenatide, liraglutide, lixisenatide, tirzepatide) that reported at least one cancer outcome (thyroid, pancreatic, colorectal, gastric, esophageal, liver, gallbladder, breast, ovarian, endometrial, kidney; multiple myeloma; meningioma).

Two reviewers independently screened and extracted, with a third adjudicating.

Risk of bias used Cochrane RoB 2, certainty used GRADE (four levels), publication bias used funnel plots plus Egger's test. Pooling used random effects (Hartung–Knapp–Sidik–Jonkman) with odds ratios (a close approximation to relative risk for rare cancers) and I² for heterogeneity. Because many trials had zero events in a group, they applied a treatment-arm continuity correction to reduce zero-event bias.

Preplanned subgroups covered follow-up and treatment duration, population (T2DM vs. obesity), weight-loss potential, dose, duration of action, drug class, and medullary vs. papillary thyroid strata, plus sensitivity analyses (dropping moderate/high-RoB trials, restricting to semaglutide/tirzepatide, and >156-week follow-up).

Key results

48 trials, 94,245 participants (51,521 on drug, 42,724 on placebo), median follow-up only 70 weeks, 43 rated low risk of bias. Every primary outcome points to "little or no effect": thyroid cancer 0.14% vs 0.07%, OR 1.37 (95% CI 0.82–2.31); pancreatic OR 0.84 (0.53–1.35); breast 0.95 (0.60–1.49); kidney 1.12 (0.78–1.60) — these four at moderate certainty.

Colorectal, esophageal, liver, gallbladder, ovarian, endometrial, multiple myeloma, and meningioma are low-certainty no-effect; gastric is "very uncertain." Thyroid stratified by histology showed no medullary-vs-papillary group difference (P=0.66), with low heterogeneity throughout.

Absolute risk differences work out to a handful of cases more or fewer per 10,000 treated — clinically near zero. One counter-signal is flagged: colorectal cancer was numerically higher, with a trend toward increased risk in trials beyond 156 weeks.

How solid is the evidence

The strength is the design: randomized trials only, which keeps observational confounding and healthy-user bias out, plus per-cancer RoB2 + GRADE grading and consistent subgroup/sensitivity results — the most credible tier in this literature. The softness, which the authors spell out, is twofold.

First, these trials were never designed for cancer; it was an exploratory safety endpoint with adjudication varying across trials, few events, and follow-up typically 1–3 years — far shorter than solid-tumor latency, so "no effect" really reads as "no signal detectable over this short a window," insensitive to both harm and benefit.

Second, without patient-level data they can't verify that randomization actually balanced baseline cancer risk (family history, smoking, prior neoplasms).

They put it honestly: the study supports the absence of an increased cancer risk but was neither designed nor powered to confirm a protective effect. So it falsifies the observational optimism without proving GLP-1RAs prevent cancer.

entry.title: GLP-1 Receptor Agonists and Cancer Risk in Adults With Obesity
entry.content:
Among obese/overweight adults eligible for anti-obesity drugs, GLP-1RA users had about one-sixth lower overall cancer incidence than nonusers (HR 0.83); endometrial, ovarian, and meningioma fell the most, while kidney cancer showed a rising signal.

The authors
