The lead and corresponding author is Jiang Bian (Department of Biostatistics and Health Data Science, Indiana University School of Medicine, and the Regenstrief Institute; previously at the University of Florida), whose work focuses on health informatics and real-world data. Hao Dai and Yongqiu Li are co–first authors.

The group builds and uses the OneFlorida+ Clinical Research Network, a PCORnet regional data network, combining EHR pharmacoepidemiology with AI and informatics methods. Co-authors include pharmacoepidemiologists Yi Guo and Jingchuan Guo, oncologist Thomas J. George, and metabolism and biochemistry researcher Ramon C. Sun.

The abstract was presented at the 2025 ASCO Annual Meeting, and the full paper appeared in JAMA Oncology.

The problem

GLP-1RAs, including semaglutide, liraglutide, and tirzepatide, began as glucose-lowering drugs and have been approved for chronic weight management since 2014. Their long-term association with cancer incidence remains unsettled as prescribing expands.

Previous studies were largely confined to people with type 2 diabetes and compared GLP-1RAs with other glucose-lowering drugs. They could not directly answer what happens among the broader population eligible for anti-obesity medication, with or without diabetes, relative to nonuse. Most studies also did not examine heterogeneity of treatment effects.

The remaining gap was a design that covered the real prescribing population while controlling confounding and immortal time bias.

The approach

The study used target trial emulation: specify the eligibility criteria, treatment strategies, outcomes, and time zero of an ideal randomized trial, then approximate that trial with observational data to reduce confounding and immortal time bias. OneFlorida+ supplied longitudinal EHRs for about 20 million people across 14 health-care organizations from 2014 to 2024.

Eligible adults had BMI ≥30, or BMI 27–29.9 with at least one weight-related comorbidity. The study excluded people younger than 18, those with previous cancer, and those pregnant at entry.

Exposure was a first GLP-1RA prescription identified through RxNorm. Contemporaneous nonusers were aligned with users within a ±1-week window so that both groups had comparable index dates.

The analysis used 1:1 time-dependent propensity-score matching on demographic characteristics, three-year comorbidity history, one-year medication history, and laboratory measurements. Covariate balance was assessed with standardized mean differences, using SMD<0.1 as the target. An SMD at or above 0.1 would indicate residual imbalance and weaken interpretation of subsequent risk comparisons.

Outcomes were the incidence of 14 cancers, comprising 13 obesity-associated cancers and lung cancer. Cox models estimated HRs, and Fine–Gray models addressed death as a competing risk. A doubly robust meta-learner with triple cross-fitting estimated individualized treatment effects, followed by SHAP values and a single decision tree to identify potential benefit and harm subgroups.

Key results

After matching, the cohort contained 86,632 adults: 43,317 GLP-1RA users and 43,315 nonusers. Mean age was 52 years, 68% were women, about half had type 2 diabetes, and nearly half had BMI ≥30.

The pooled incidence across 14 cancers was 13.6 versus 16.4 per 1,000 person-years, with an overall HR of 0.83 (95% CI, 0.76–0.91; P=.002). This supports a lower overall incidence among GLP-1RA users, while remaining an observational association.

Site-specific reductions were reported for endometrial cancer (HR 0.75; 95% CI, 0.57–0.99), ovarian cancer (HR 0.53; 95% CI, 0.29–0.96), and meningioma (HR 0.69; 95% CI, 0.48–0.97). The composite gynecologic endpoint was more stable, with an HR of 0.68 (95% CI, 0.52–0.87).

Kidney cancer showed an elevated signal, with an HR of 1.38 (95% CI, 0.99–1.93), particularly among people younger than 65 or those with overweight rather than obesity. Because the CI crosses the null, independent confirmation is required.

High-ranking SHAP features for the kidney-cancer ITE included heart failure, metformin, insulin, proton-pump inhibitors, and substance use. Fine–Gray results were directionally consistent with the primary analysis.

How solid is the evidence

Target trial emulation, a new-user design, propensity-score matching, and competing-risk analysis all strengthen the primary comparison. They cannot eliminate every source of bias in an observational study.

The first limitation is confounding by indication and unmeasured confounding. Prescribing is related to health behavior, health-care intensity, and ability to pay, which EHR data may not capture fully.

BMI and glycemic control were not modeled longitudinally in a way that separates the drug from weight loss or improved glycemia. Because weight loss and glycemia may be mediators, routine covariate adjustment could also introduce overadjustment.

The second limitation is multiplicity. The three significant site-specific results have P values around .04–.05 and CI upper bounds of 0.96–0.99, with no reported multiplicity adjustment. The composite gynecologic endpoint is more stable than the borderline single-cancer estimates.

Rare-cancer event counts were small, CIs were wide, and follow-up was short relative to tumor latency. These results are therefore best treated as signals for further validation rather than established causal effects.

entry.title: Diabetes and Cancer: A Consensus Report
entry.content:
Diabetes really is linked to several cancers, but the mechanism and the causal role of glucose-lowering drugs were still unsettled in 2010 — this ADA/ACS consensus mapped the battlefield and honestly fenced off every unknown.

The authors

The writing group was co-chaired by Edward Giovannucci, a nutrition/epidemiology researcher at the Harvard School of Public Health, and David Harlan, a diabetes physician at UMass.

Members included Michael Pollak (McGill, a long-time researcher on insulin/IGF and cancer), Susan Gapstur (American Cancer Society), and Laurel Habel (Kaiser Permanente Division of Research).

The report was written independently by the group after a consensus conference convened jointly by the American Diabetes Association (ADA) and the American Cancer Society (ACS) in December 2009.

The problem

Diabetes and cancer are both common and rising, and they co-occur in the same person more often than chance would predict. In 2009 a batch of analyses suggesting the long-acting insulin analog glargine might raise cancer risk caused an uproar, which is why the ADA and ACS sat down together to turn a scattered literature into one panorama.

The report splits into four questions: is there a meaningful association between diabetes and cancer incidence/prognosis, what risk factors do the two diseases share, what are the possible biologic links, and do diabetes treatments change cancer risk or prognosis. What was missing wasn't data points but a framework that could weigh association, mechanism, confounding, and drug signals together.

The approach

This is not an empirical study but a structured synthesis of the existing literature — the method is the consensus review itself. What has value is its analytical frame.

For every association the authors force themselves to separate three explanations: diabetes causes cancer directly (e.g., hyperglycemia itself), diabetes is merely a marker of underlying biology (insulin resistance, hyperinsulinemia), or the two are dragged together by shared risk factors (obesity, inactivity, diet) — the third being confounding.

Mechanistically they bet on the insulin/IGF axis: hyperinsulinemia drives proliferation and anti-apoptosis through the insulin receptor and IGF-I, insulin also lowers hepatic IGFBP-1/2 to raise free IGF-I, and it lowers SHBG to raise the bioavailability of sex steroids.

Hyperglycemia (the Warburg effect) is explicitly downgraded — in type 1 diabetes animal models tumors actually grow more slowly, so hyperglycemia alone doesn't drive growth.

The drug section is where the craft shows: metformin, TZDs, sulfonylureas, incretins, insulin/analogs are each walked through, and each flags the traps of observational studies — most sharply confounding by indication (metformin tends to go to patients with short diabetes duration and no liver/kidney disease) and comparator choice (is the sulfonylurea "increase" the drug causing cancer, or the metformin in the comparator arm protecting?).

Key results

The direction and magnitude of the associations are the most concrete part. Relative to non-diabetics, diabetes raises liver, pancreatic, and endometrial cancer to about twofold or higher, colorectal, breast, and bladder to 1.2–1.5-fold, prostate cancer is lower, and lung is essentially unrelated.

Reverse causality for pancreatic cancer (abnormal glucose may be a consequence of the tumor) is handled by restricting to diabetes preceding diagnosis by at least 5 years, and the association survives; the lower prostate risk holds both before and after PSA screening spread, so it isn't detection bias. For prognosis, breast cancer with diabetes carries a 5-year mortality HR of about 1.39.

On risk factors, the Diabetes Prevention Program's intensive lifestyle arm cut diabetes incidence by 58%, most of it from weight loss; bariatric surgery drops cancer risk in obese women to RR 0.58–0.62 (mostly breast and endometrial), with no association in men.

Drug conclusions are restrained: metformin tracks with lower cancer risk in observational studies; TZDs are inconsistent (a rosiglitazone trial meta-analysis shows no significant change); sulfonylureas show some elevation relative to metformin; glargine has higher IGF-1R affinity and mitogenic potency than human insulin, but a 5-year glargine-vs-NPH RCT saw no excess (57 vs 62 cases), while the larger ORIGIN trial was underway but powered for cardiovascular endpoints.

How solid is the evidence

This is a narrative consensus; its strength rides on the underlying studies, not on new data it generates. Incidence associations for common cancers are fairly stable (consistent across cohorts), but the causal reading is left wide open — direct carcinogen, marker, or shared confounding, the authors don't settle it. The softest part is the drugs.

They honestly list the traps: confounding by indication, comparator choice, too few site-specific cancer events for adequate power, follow-up too short for newer drugs.

But the era limited their tools: this 2010 paper predates Suissa's 2012 systematic critique of immortal time bias in metformin studies — only afterward did the field realize the "metformin is anti-cancer" observational signal was substantially inflated by immortal time and other time-related biases. So its optimism about metformin should be discounted today.

Its repeated insistence on not pooling all cancers (a null lung association dilutes real signals) does hold up.

entry.title: Introduction to Causal Directed Acyclic Graphs
entry.content:
A Stanford intro-to-DAGs teaching deck: confounding, colliders, mediators, backdoor paths, minimal adjustment sets, all done cleanly and to standard.

The authors

A solo teaching presentation by Amber W. Trickey, senior biostatistician at Stanford's S-SPIRE (Surgery Policy Improvement Research and Education Center), where she supports surgical and health-services research teams in study design and analysis.

The problem

Up front: this is a slide deck, not a research paper. The author is a senior biostatistician at Stanford S-SPIRE (Surgery Policy Improvement Research and Education), presenting in January 2019 to the group's Works in Progress for surgical and health-services researchers — clinicians and statisticians.

Their data is almost entirely observational (Medicare, HCUP, NSQIP, Optum); treatment isn't randomized, it's set by the often-unknown mechanisms of routine care.

The teaching problem is plain: when you can't randomize and correlation ≠ causation, how do you decide in a principled way which variables to adjust for. The answer is the DAG — draw your causal assumptions, then use the picture to see where bias comes from, whether the effect is identifiable, and which covariates to pick.

The approach

The content follows a standard arc. Motivation first: DAGs display sources of bias, decide whether an effect is identifiable from the available data, and make assumptions explicit (the author is honest that analytic models carry assumptions anyway — the DAG just surfaces them).

Then the rules: directed and acyclic, time flowing left to right, A for exposure, Y for outcome, C for covariates, U for unmeasured, with parent/child/ancestor/descendant defined one by one.

The core is a four-step build:

  • Step 1, articulate the research question and place A, Y, and a "?"; step 2, consider the mediators and moderators embedded in it;
  • step 3, add confounders (common causes of A and Y); step 4, fill in other relevant variables.

The inclusion rule: every common cause of any two nodes goes in, including unmeasured ones and selection variables; variables causing Y but not A are optional.

Then adjustment — backdoor paths, colliders (two arrows colliding at a common descendant), and the three iron rules of conditioning: conditioning on a variable in an open backdoor path removes the non-causal association, conditioning on a collider instead opens the path it was blocking, and conditioning on a mediator strips out part of the real effect.

It closes by folding confounding, collider, and selection bias (including Berkson's) into one structural family, points to DAGitty, and ends on the AHRQ Sauer-VanderWeele summary: build the DAG independent of the available data, include all common causes, and find a minimal set that blocks every backdoor without inadvertently opening a collider.

Key results

It's teaching material, so the "results" are the rules and examples it lands. The three conditioning rules are stated cleanly — the most valuable slide in the deck.

The appendicitis example is from the author's own JAMA Surgery paper (A = nonoperative management, Y = 30-day ED visits, confounders age/gender/insurance/severity/language, income unmeasured): a clean pure-confounding case with no mediators, moderators, or colliders, where conditioning still leaves residual confounding from language and income.

The low-back-pain surgery example exists to demonstrate a collider: conditioning on "surgery," a common effect, opens collider bias. One positive, one cautionary — exactly the point that you don't dump every variable into the regression. The tool is DAGitty; the closing principle is the minimal adjustment set.

How solid is the evidence

It isn't evidence, it's a lecture, so the only fair questions are accuracy, clarity, and coverage.

Accuracy is fine and current: treating selection bias as a special case of collider bias (the Hernán/Greenland structural view) is right, the three conditioning rules are exact, the "build the DAG independent of the data" point is the correct emphasis, and it even flags that "adjusting/controlling" is misleading language and one should say "conditioning" (Shrier-Platt).

The weakness is coverage, which is what matters for a reader like me. No formal definition of d-separation, no named backdoor criterion, no do-calculus or front-door, and IV appears only as notation.

The deck teaches colliders but does not cover M-bias or butterfly bias, a common adjustment trap. Time-varying confounding and g-methods are also outside its scope. It acknowledges disagreement over whether effect modifiers belong in a DAG but leaves the issue unresolved.

DAGitty's algorithmic capabilities, including minimal sufficient sets and testable implications, are compressed into a brief instruction to save the code. As a 2019 introduction, the material is sound but stops before the harder methodological problems.

entry.title: Causal inference on human behaviour
entry.content:
A Nature Human Behaviour conceptual overview: it sorts the difficulties of causal inference on behaviour into seven challenges, notes that fixing one usually worsens another, and lands its hopes on triangulation plus asking sharper questions.

The authors

An interdisciplinary group led by Drew H. Bailey of UC Irvine's School of Education, with senior author Kou Murayama, an educational psychologist at the University of Tübingen. Co-authors include Konrad Kording (Penn) and Julia Rohrer, who studies causal inference in psychology.

The problem

Behavioural science asks "what causes what," but behaviour sits inside a complex causal system we observe only in part. Variables like happiness or cognitive skill mostly can't be randomized, every method carries its own bias, and in several fields (psychology especially) researchers often haven't settled whether they even want a causal effect.

The authors' read: causal-inference tools have advanced fast and longitudinal data are more available, but the advances are scattered across economics, epidemiology, political science, psychology and sociology, each patching its own corner, with no single map that lays the challenges and the ways out side by side. This Review is that map — non-technical, cross-disciplinary, focused on longitudinal data.

The approach

The framework comes in two halves.

First, seven challenges, named as in the paper. (1) Questions that aren't specific enough: "why are some people happier" is a reverse causal question, "does happiness predict health" is only prediction, and only "the returns to an extra year of schooling" is both specific and forward-causal.

(2) Under-control: you can essentially never prove all confounders have been adjusted for. (3) Over-control: conditioning on a mediator or collider introduces post-treatment bias — in police-stop data, "being stopped" is a collider that makes racial discrimination look smaller.

(4) Effect heterogeneity: an average treatment effect hides variation, and under some conditions the average itself is biased. (5) Interference/spillover: peers and siblings affect each other, breaking SUTVA. (6) Timescales: effects have latency and may be non-stationary.

(7) "Fat-handed" interventions: you can't nudge only happiness without also moving mood, and behavioural constructs are often ill-defined and not directly manipulable.

The through-line: a method optimized to fix one problem usually worsens another — add controls to fight under-control and you risk over-control. No free lunch.

The second half is the way out, four strategies: clarify the question and usually narrow it; once it's set, choose the design and variables and mind construct measurement; treat "causal informativeness" as a continuum rather than a binary switch, instead of refusing to say "causal" just because the data are observational; and triangulate (Fig.

1) — put the strong estimate from a well-defined but probably fat-handed intervention next to the observational estimate, read convergence or divergence against theory, and revise theory, using within-study experimental-versus-observational comparison, coherent pattern matching, robustness and falsification tests, bounding/sensitivity analysis, and structural models.

Key results

A methods/opinion piece — the output is a taxonomy and recommendations, not numbers. The useful artifacts are the seven-challenge list, the four strategies, and the Fig. 1 loop where intervention estimates, observational estimates and theory mutually check and revise one another; Boxes 2–8 pair each challenge with tentative solutions and further reading.

A few concrete claims worth keeping: state the study's goal (causal estimation) and the certainty a method affords separately, rather than lumping both under "causal"; and structural models get a positive example — Todd and Wolpin's model of school attendance predicted how much a subsidy would raise attendance, later confirmed by a randomized experiment.

The authors are blunt that triangulation won't cleanly answer the broad question, but can answer narrower ones more solidly.

How solid is the evidence

As a Review it's honest but limited. No new method, no data, no validation; the diagnosis is sharp, but the prescriptions are mostly tools the field already has, labelled "tentative." Its triangulation is a reasoning stance, not an algorithm: no criterion for how much convergence counts, nothing on how to weight several biased estimates.

Worse, whether two approaches' biases point the same way or opposite is itself a judgment call — the authors themselves warn that if controls are poor and interventions all fat-handed, the net bias of both may go the same direction, and then agreement stops being evidence. On DAGs, good/bad controls and colliders, this is textbook for anyone who knows causal graphs.

entry.title: Using Causal Diagrams for Biomedical Research
entry.content:
A clinician-facing DAG primer: use the confounder/collider/mediator rules to decide which variables go into a multivariable model and which stay out, then let Framingham numbers show how the wrong choice miscalculates the effect.

The authors

Led by Demetrios N. Kyriacou, professor of emergency and preventive medicine at Northwestern's Feinberg School of Medicine, with Northwestern cardiovascular epidemiologist Philip Greenland and Mohammad A. Mansournia of Tehran University of Medical Sciences, who publishes widely on causal-inference methods.

The problem

Picking covariates in clinical research, many people still lean on mechanical algorithms — stepwise, significance, change-in-estimate — letting software pick the set that fits best. The trouble is these methods optimize overall fit and never ask what role each variable plays in the causal structure.

That yields two systematic errors: leaving out a confounder that belongs in, and putting in colliders or mediators that don't.

Both bias the estimate, through different mechanisms. What the authors want is for researchers, working with statisticians, to be able to say from a causal diagram why this variable is in and that one is out, instead of outsourcing variable selection to an algorithm. An old problem, but worth restating in a clinical (emergency-medicine) journal.

The approach

The core move is to treat the DAG as the basis for variable selection. A DAG is directed (arrows follow time order) and acyclic (no feedback loops); a path is any unbroken route along or against arrows, and a causal path is a run of arrows all pointing the same way. Each variable type has its own rule:

  • Confounder, three criteria: an extraneous risk factor for the outcome, independent of the exposure; associated with the exposure in the source population (directly or via a common cause); not affected by exposure or outcome, and never on the causal path between them. If it qualifies, adjust.
  • A collider is the common effect of two variables, and conditioning on it manufactures an association out of nothing (collider / selection bias), so exclude it; a mediator sits on the causal path, and adjusting it with ordinary regression erases the part of the effect that flows through it, so you need a formal mediation analysis to split direct and indirect effects.

To find a sufficient adjustment set, use backdoor paths: start from an arrow into the exposure (the backdoor), follow an open path (no colliders) to the outcome, and block every backdoor path by adjusting at least one variable on it. The authors argue for using the conventional three criteria and the backdoor method together.

Running through it all is one line: explanatory modeling is not predictive modeling, so don't judge by mechanical selection or change-in-estimate alone.

Key results

The demonstration uses Framingham, 4,287 people, exposure diabetes, outcome cardiac ischemia. Table 1 adds variables step by step: unadjusted HR 2.56; adjusting obesity alone drops it to 2.32 (9.4% change, obesity is a confounder); adding age drops it to 2.08 (another 10.3%, age is a confounder too). So far this is confounding removal.

The trap is step three: put in MACE (major adverse cardiovascular events) and the HR plunges from 2.08 to 1.28 (a 38.5% drop), the CI crossing 1. But MACE is a common effect of diabetes and cardiac ischemia — a collider, not a confounder — so that 38.5% looks like confounding removal and is actually collider bias introduced.

The same "estimate moved" event is right for obesity and age, wrong for MACE, and change-in-estimate can't tell them apart.

The mediation case is the twin: obesity on cardiac ischemia unadjusted 1.61, ordinary regression on hypertension drops it to 1.45 (9.9%), but hypertension is a mediator, so that drop erases the indirect effect; a formal mediation analysis gives total 1.69, direct 1.52, indirect 1.11 (direct × indirect ≈ total).

How solid is the evidence

It's a tutorial, not a study, so "solid" means how honest it is — and the authors are honest.

They draw the limits themselves: a DAG depends on prior scientific knowledge that is often incomplete or unavailable; whether a variable is a confounder, collider, or mediator depends on temporal order and measurement timing; a diagram that omits variables or arrows is oversimplified, and the figures here are deliberately so ("include arrows when in doubt").

They flag real problems: a variable can be a confounder and a collider at once (M-bias); the definition of confounding has been argued over for decades, with the conventional and backdoor criteria only largely overlapping; and every variable here is dichotomized, which itself introduces residual confounding. The diagram's power is only as good as the data and the prior knowledge behind it.

entry.title: Assessing causality in epidemiology: revisiting Bradford Hill to incorporate developments in causal thinking
entry.content:
Hill's nine causal viewpoints laid one by one against DAGs, sufficient-component-cause (SCC) models and GRADE — seeing which hold up, which get rewritten along triangulation/negative-control lines, and which should retire. The common substrate is the potential-outcomes framework.

The authors

Senior author Srinivasa Vittal Katikireddi is a public health professor at Glasgow's MRC/CSO Social and Public Health Sciences Unit; lead author Michal Shimonovich, at the same unit, studies causal assessment in evidence synthesis. Co-author Katherine Keyes is a Columbia University epidemiologist.

The problem

Bradford Hill's nine 1965 viewpoints are still the default tool for judging causality in epidemiology. Hill called them viewpoints and warned against ticking them off as a checklist, yet half a century of practice has treated them as exactly that. In the same half-century, causal inference grew the potential-outcomes framework plus three approaches that operationalise it: DAGs, SCC models (causal pies), and GRADE.

Whether the old and the new overlap, and whether the new can explain the old, had not been compared systematically. Some had put SCC against DAGs, one had put GRADE against Hill, but nobody had laid DAGs and SCC together against all nine viewpoints, and DAGs are precisely what the field has leaned on most.

The question: looking back at Hill through modern causal frameworks, which viewpoints have solid theoretical grounding, which are just the same thing said differently, and which should retire.

The approach

The method is plain: draw a DAG and an SCC pie for each Hill viewpoint, map each against every GRADE domain, and use one running toy example (alcohol → active-TB, with overcrowding as the confounder) to show what each viewpoint "looks like" under the three frameworks.

The key is that all three build on potential outcomes, and the shared currency is exchangeability: DAGs tell you which variables to condition on to get there; SCC models stress how unknown components make exchangeability harder to judge; GRADE simply ranks evidence by how far a design secures exchangeability (RCTs above NRSs).

The fate of the nine sorts into four groups.

  • Four hold up directly: strength, temporality, plausibility, experiment. Strength via DAG bias analysis, SCC showing how competing causes dilute the RR, GRADE offering an RR 2–5 threshold; temporality explicitly encoded by DAGs, which can also probe reverse causation (TB before/after diagnosis); plausibility made explicit by DAGs (mediation, causal chain) and SCC (interaction); experiment via DAGs explaining why randomisation buys exchangeability (randomisation = instrumental variable), extending to natural experiments and the target trial.
  • Specificity gets rewritten. In epidemiology one cause has many effects and one effect many causes, so specificity almost never holds; the authors swap it for falsification / negative controls (head lice as a placebo outcome), which is what remains useful in it.
  • Consistency gets split — the viewpoint closest to triangulation. Hill said consistent results from different approaches strengthen causation; following GRADE, the authors push back: consistency may just be the same bias pulling everything the same way. So it should be split in two — transportability (does the effect hold under a different population/context; DAGs for confounding structure, SCC for competing causes) and unexplained heterogeneity (unexplained statistical inconsistency, which is what actually undermines causation).
  • Largely retired: dose-response is downgraded (a gradient can be manufactured by confounding, and threshold effects are common); coherence and analogy are judged "utility not clearly supported" — the former is indistinguishable from plausibility, the latter gets conflated by GRADE with directness.

To be clear: the three frameworks the paper explicitly compares are DAG / SCC / GRADE; it does not list "triangulation" as one of them. The triangulation logic is buried inside the rewrite of consistency.

Key results

The output is two tables — a mapping table (Table 4) and a conclusions table (Table 5) — not numbers. The core finding: four of the nine genuinely overlap — strength (including analysis of plausible confounding), temporality, plausibility (DAGs encode mediation, SCC encodes interaction), experiment (including how study design bears on exchangeability).

Consistency should be split into transportability vs unexplained heterogeneity; specificity is too rare, so falsification / negative controls serve better; dose-response is less useful than assumed, since confounding alone can produce a gradient; coherence and analogy are of doubtful utility.

On triangulation they never use the word, but the consistency passage is blunt: pointing to the same conclusion does not mean the results are statistically consistent or poolable, and agreement may just be shared bias — which is exactly the starting point of Lawlor/Davey Smith triangulation, only approached here from the Hill side.

The magnitude demonstration sits in the SCC table (alcohol alone RR 3.0, overcrowding alone 3.5 vs 2.0 across two populations, both together 4.5), showing how competing causes and reference-group prevalence shift the RR.

How solid is the evidence

This is an essay, and the authors keep saying "preliminary" and "tentative." Several limits I accept:

  • A single deliberately simplified alcohol → TB toy example can't carry a general claim that "every viewpoint works this way in real studies"; swap in a messy, poorly-measured, many-confounder problem and the mapping blurs.
  • They compare only the three "best-known" frameworks (DAG / SCC / GRADE), explicitly skipping IARC, teratogenicity criteria and others, and traditions outside potential outcomes don't enter.
  • They themselves waver on whether SCC belongs, conceding it is more a description of causal reality and the least used.
  • The judgments are qualitative: which viewpoint "survives," which is "utility not clearly supported," is largely the authors' reading, not a reproducible test.

To their credit the boundaries are drawn honestly — no overselling this as a "new set of causal criteria," just an opening bid inviting argument.

entry.title: Can we believe the DAGs? A comment on the relationship between causal DAGs and mechanisms
entry.content:
Real causation is a mechanism running in continuous time, yet a DAG is built on sparse discrete observations. Coarsen the sampling and the DAG sprouts direct arrows that don't exist, collapsing into the transitive closure of the underlying mechanism graph. For anyone building DAG tooling, this is the cold water to read first.

The authors

Lead author Odd O. Aalen is Professor of Medical Statistics at the University of Oslo, known for the Aalen additive-hazards model. With Oslo colleagues Røysland and Gran, his group develops continuous-time, stochastic-process approaches to causal inference — the mechanistic view this paper sets against discrete DAGs.

The problem

Causal DAGs are the lingua franca of epidemiology: draw nodes, connect arrows, use d-separation and the backdoor criterion to decide what's identifiable. But what is a node? In most applications it's a single measurement of some quantity at one time point.

The real mechanism, say aspirin suppressing platelet aggregation or HAART driving down viral load, runs continuously in time. The authors' question is blunt: if you sample a continuous process at a few discrete times and draw a DAG from that, does the DAG still match the underlying mechanism? Their answer is mostly no, and nothing warns you where it breaks.

This isn't a rejection of DAGs, which remain good for reasoning about selection bias and confounding; it's cold water on using DAGs to represent mechanism.

The approach

The core move is to write the mechanism explicitly as a continuous-time dynamical system, then see what it projects to under discrete sampling. They take a linear stochastic differential equation, dX = [A(μ−X)+a]dt + σdW, as the true mechanism; the off-diagonal entries of matrix A encode who directly drives whom, with A_ij = 0 meaning X_i is locally independent of X_j.

This "local independence graph" is the natural lift of Bayesian-network conditional independence into continuous time, defined via martingale theory, and it holds what a DAG can't: feedback loops (CD4 is both affected by treatment and determines when treatment starts, two arrows in opposite directions, not acyclic at all).

The crux is the gap between A and its matrix exponential e^{−tA}. A is local dependence at an infinitesimal instant; e^{−tA} is dependence over a finite interval of length t; they are not the same object. As soon as the sampling gap isn't infinitesimal, local dynamics get "smeared out": a true E→M→D chain, observed only at 0 and t, makes a regression report a direct E→D effect.

The appendix turns this into a general proposition: the time-discrete DAG is the transitive closure of the local independence graph, and every node reachable in the mechanism graph gets a direct arrow in the discrete DAG. Discretization doesn't just blur the picture, it actively inserts spurious direct edges. The one assumption it leans on is faithfulness-flavored: the two indirect paths don't exactly cancel.

Key results

The aspirin simulation is the one that stings. The true mechanism routes 100% of the effect through platelet aggregation, yet a mediation analysis on discrete observations reports a mediated proportion of only 17%–23% in setup 1, conjuring nearly 80% of "direct effect" out of nothing.

More counterintuitively, the slower treatment acts on the mediator, the closer the estimated mediated proportion gets to the truth (56%–74% in setup 2, 60%–92% in setup 3); the authors admit this surprised them, since fast-acting aspirin gives the worst picture.

The second simulation is harsher: the true mechanism is a chain X1→X2→X3→X4, but discrete regression hands the root cause X1 a negative coefficient (partial correlation −0.104), the wrong sign, and acting on it would be a mistake.

Real data reproduces it: in the Swiss HIV Cohort, monthly data cleanly recovers RNA→CD4→HEM with no direct RNA→HEM edge; drop to yearly or tri-yearly resolution and the chain smears, a spurious direct edge appears.

One corollary in passing: if the exposure is itself a continuous process, the instrumental-variable exclusion restriction breaks and IV estimates are biased.

How solid is the evidence

This is a commentary and cautionary piece, so watch the boundaries. First, "the true mechanism is a linear SDE" is carrying a lot; real mechanisms are usually non-linear, and the authors concede linearity is just for tractability, though the qualitative "discretization fills in arrows" result is fairly robust to that.

Second, the transitive-closure claim hangs on a faithfulness-like assumption, and the authors repeatedly stress this is "mainly a problem for coarse discretization," since fine sampling recovers the structure.

So what's actually indicted is not DAGs per se but the naive, time-blind use of DAGs on coarsely-sampled data. Third, the remedy they end on (a series of DAGs defined continuously over time, dynamic path models, additive hazards models) is still DAGs, just with time made explicit, of a piece with Hernán–Robins time-varying DAGs and g-methods.

So the title is more provocative than the content: the honest answer is "yes, if you respect time and sampling." Fourth, in the simulations they get to know the true A; in real life you don't, so the bite of the critique depends on whether "continuous-time mechanism" is the right model of reality, and where a phenomenon really is driven by discrete decision points the blade cuts less.

entry.title: Directed Acyclic Graph Guidance Documentation: Human System Risk Board
entry.content:
The operating manual for how NASA's Human System Risk Board hand-builds, reviews, and version-controls its ~30 risk causal DAGs.

The authors

Authored by NASA's Human System Risk Board team led by Erik L. Antonsen, who chairs the board and trained in both aerospace engineering (PhD) and medicine (MD). This NASA technical memorandum sets standardized guidance for risk teams building and updating DAGs.

The problem

NASA tracks about 30 astronaut health and performance risks, all deriving from five unchangeable spaceflight Hazards (altered gravity, radiation, isolation and confinement, hostile closed environment, distance from Earth).

These risks were historically studied in silos, which missed the real situation: injury to the human system in spaceflight is multifactorial and cumulative, stacking from launch onward and sometimes across a crew member's post-career life.

The Board used to score each risk on its own with a Likelihood×Consequence rating and a red/yellow/green light — scoring that can't see the synergies between risks, where a minor issue harmless on its own gets amplified in another body system or at a later time.

In January 2020 the HSRB began a pilot to use DAGs to turn "how risk flows causally" into a shared mental model across all stakeholders. This TM is the guidance for how risk-custodian teams build and update those graphs.

The approach

The core isn't an algorithm; it's a set of conventions plus governance. Every risk DAG starts at one or more Hazards and ends at one or more Mission Level Outcomes, with contributing factors in between; each arrow is defined as a falsifiable causal hypothesis that can be added, removed, or redrawn as evidence changes.

Graphs come in two tiers: Narrative DAGs for communication, reviewed by the Board and configuration-managed; Detailed DAGs, finer-grained and meant for network analysis, which are too specialized to go before the Board.

To compose the individual graphs into one network, terminology must be harmonized and neutral — for "altered gravity" alone, 10 groups had used 6 different names, so names are forced to a common list and continuous variables are preferred. Feedback loops are banned; where genuinely needed, they are unrolled with time-indexed nodes. First drafts are drawn in Dagitty.

The whole thing runs on crowdsourcing plus configuration management: custodian teams draft, the HSRB (10 standing members, 100–200 attendees per meeting) runs a formal review and comment-resolution, and disagreements are settled by the Board or its Chair against evidence standards.

Key results

The deliverable is a set of reusable components.

The node vocabulary is pre-defined: 5 Hazards and 8 Mission Level Outcomes fix the two ends; graph theory's exogenous/endogenous nodes are renamed External/Integral Factors; countermeasures split into monitoring, prevention, and intervention, each with hard drawing rules — a prevention points at the node it is meant to stop, an intervention is not drawn back to the treated condition but to its downstream node.

There are also category nodes with nesting, grey Risk nodes drawn with incoming links only, decision nodes, and time nodes like Effective Exposure Duration. An edge carries two properties: a Level of Evidence (LOE) and a strength of influence (path coefficients or joint-probability tables where data exist).

LOE comes from six Bradford Hill criteria on a four-tier scale with inverted numbering — 4 Speculative, 3 Weak, 2 Moderate, 1 Strong; Speculative = Temporality + Analogy is the threshold for inclusion; each source first passes a quality gate, and poor methodology caps at Weak.

Dotted lines mean weak/speculative, solid lines moderate/strong. A node qualifies if it meets Speculative and has at least one link, or on documented SME concern. New terms must be filed with the office, discussed at the Board, and signed off by the Chair; by January 13, 2022, initial DAGs for all 30 risks had passed.

How solid is the evidence

Its value is process auditability: configuration management plus a comment-resolution trail is a traceable organizational memory of why the graph looks the way it does, and every edge is written as a falsifiable hypothesis. But the rigor rests almost entirely on people.

LOE is assigned by hand, the quality gate is a per-paper human judgment, and Bradford Hill is a qualitative heuristic with only four levels and no automated grading; "SME concern" can justify inclusion on its own — honest, but also the hardest thing to audit.

The strength-of-influence layer is mostly vaporware: the text repeatedly says path coefficients go in "if sufficient data are available," while in practice most edges carry only an LOE and no weights, so the "Bayesian network" is potential, not present.

The authors own two weaknesses: once a DAG is baselined it can anchor the community, and how much detail to show "remains more art than science." Authority sits with the Board and Chair — reproducible inside NASA, but not a portable algorithm. And with 30 graphs taking hundreds of people two years, it does not scale.

entry.title: Causal diagramming for assessing human system risk in spaceflight
entry.content:
NASA manages roughly 30 astronaut-health risks with DAGs: experts build the graphs by hand, grade each edge by level of evidence, run them through configuration review, and stitch them into a risk network.

The authors

First author Erik Antonsen chairs NASA's Human System Risk Board and is at Baylor College of Medicine; senior author Daniel M. Buckland is at NASA Johnson Space Center and Duke University. The team applies causal diagrams (DAGs) to astronaut health risks.

The problem

NASA tracks about 30 risks to astronaut health and performance, all deriving from the 5 hazards of spaceflight, and evaluates each against 8 design reference missions (from short low-Earth-orbit trips to three-year Mars missions).

These risks were historically characterized in separate silos, which worked while the risk records were being built, but astronauts are exposed to every risk at once, and the risk itself is multifactorial and cumulative.

Siloing left different disciplines talking past each other: altered gravity alone went by six names (microgravity, weightlessness, partial gravity, and so on). With no common terminology and no shared mental model, the Human System Risk Board (HSRB) struggled to agree on which risk is more urgent and where to spend limited resources.

The evidence base makes it worse: fewer than 700 humans have ever flown, so many causal links rest on expert judgment.

What they needed was not another schematic but a representation that makes complex causality explicit and can be tracked and managed by the organization.

The approach

From January 2020 the HSRB drew each risk as a DAG: nodes are random variables, directed edges denote causality, no cycles. Every graph starts at a hazard and ends at a predefined mission-level outcome (from loss of mission objectives to loss of crew life). Each risk gets two kinds of graph.

A narrative DAG tells the high-level causal story with an accompanying written walkthrough, used to align mental models at board meetings; a detailed DAG fills in specifics but must obey the same data schema. Acyclicity is a hard constraint: if a feedback loop appears, the creators pick the most likely predecessor node and place it earlier in the chain.

Getting a node into a graph has thresholds. First there must be a logical basis that the node affects the risk; the HSRB adopted 6 of Bradford Hill's criteria to grade level of evidence, with temporality and analogy as the minimum needed to reach the lowest "speculative" level and enter the DAG. For a narrative DAG the effect must also be large enough to move a downstream node measurably.

The representation is standardized almost obsessively: nodes are circles, edges run from cause to effect, exogenous is distinguished from endogenous; countermeasures split into monitoring / prevention / intervention (the carbon-monoxide case: detector → CO level → oxygen masks); category nodes can nest sub-nodes (individual factors houses modifiable / non-modifiable).

Terminology is harmonized and named as neutral random variables — nutritional status, not inadequate nutrition — so a node is reusable across risks and across DRMs; overlapping nodes across risks are aligned by "edge work," which also guards against accidental cycles.

Governance is the heavy part.

Each risk is drafted by a risk custodian team, reviewed by the HSRB as a forum of 100 to 200 people, run through a formal tracked-comment and comment-resolution process, with disagreements settled by the board or its chair against evidence standards; only the narrative DAG is configuration-managed, the detailed one is not (too much detail, too much time).

They also piloted structural validation: a subset of the bone-fracture DAG against 4 rodent datasets, testing the conditional independencies the DAG implies, and the data largely supported the structure.

Level of evidence is drawn straight into the edge style: solid for strong, dotted for weak (the SANS DAG was approved April 2020, revised May 2022).

Finally the 30 detailed DAGs were joined through common nodes into a single risk network, which the authors admit is "beyond manual approaches" to analyze; the next step is to run it as a Bayesian network with epistemic and aleatory uncertainty built in, but that is future work.

Key results

A methods/process piece, no effect sizes — the output is a reusable graph-building standard plus a governance mechanism that actually runs.

The concrete artifacts: about 30 configuration-managed risks, 5 hazards, 8 DRMs; two DAG types per risk; 6 Hill-derived evidence criteria, with temporality + analogy as the entry floor and "speculative" as the lowest grade; three countermeasure categories; tooling moved from Dagitty to the in-house DAGvision; a 100–200-person review forum; one structural validation on 4 rodent datasets; a first risk network stitched from all 30 graphs.

Evidence grading isn't just talk — it is encoded in edge style and updated across versions (the SANS DAG's two-year revision is the example).

How solid is the evidence

What's validated here is not a hypothesis but the claim that the process is workable, so the bar sits at feasibility. The causal structure comes mostly from expert judgment, not learned from data; the authors concede the evidence base is thin and many edges are speculative.

Several real problems are named but unsolved: how to grade evidence objectively (the 6 Hill criteria are still a judgment call, and someone has to set the level boundaries), the anchoring bias the first DAG imposes, and the lack of consensus on naming and granularity, which in the end is settled by the HSRB's administrative authority declaring the "official" DAG.

The defense of acyclicity — feedback loops are never simultaneous and can be shown with time-indexed nodes — is internally coherent, but they admit that collapsing duration of exposure into a single node is a stopgap.

The 30-risk network reads more like an existence proof: it can be assembled, but quantitative analysis is explicitly future work. As engineering practice it is solid; as a causal-inference method it still stops at structured expert consensus.

entry.title: Development and application of an evidence-based directed acyclic graph to evaluate the associations between metal mixtures and cardiometabolic outcomes
entry.content:
The literal precedent for the name "evidence-based DAG": one PubMed search turned 42 reviews into a causal graph, then used to pick confounder adjustment sets.

The authors

Senior author Laura Corlin is an environmental epidemiologist at Tufts University who applies causal-inference methods to observational studies of environmental toxicants; first author Emily Riseberg (Tufts) worked in her group on urinary metals and cardiometabolic outcomes.

The problem

Environmental exposures can't be randomized — assigning people to a toxic metal is unethical — so you're left with observational data, which needs confounding adjustment. A DAG is the standard tool for drawing the causal structure and reading an adjustment set off it.

But environmental epidemiology has almost no "evidence-based" DAGs: building one takes a literature review, and no one had empirically tested whether a literature-built DAG actually fits data.

For metal mixtures and cardiometabolic outcomes, none had ever been published. The authors fill two gaps at once: a method to build a DAG from the literature and test it, and the specific metal-mixture DAG itself.

The approach

Five metals: arsenic, cadmium, manganese, uranium, tungsten. Three outcomes: cardiovascular disease, fasting glucose, systolic blood pressure. One design decision comes first — force an arc from every metal to every outcome, because those associations are the ones they intend to test.

The graph's evidence comes from a single PubMed search: MeSH terms for the five metals AND the three outcomes AND (review / systematic review / meta-analysis), restricted to 2011–2021, English, human studies, plus five articles added from the authors' prior knowledge — 42 in total (As n=22, Cd n=17 the most common).

From each they extracted authors, year, journal, population, exposure, exposure sources, outcome, and main results, then wired the graph in DAGitty.

The rest of the nodes were added in four steps: identify exposure sources from the literature (diet, air, smoking, soil, drinking water); identify outcome risk factors from consensus statements (sex, age, obesity, education, ethnicity, income, physical activity, alcohol); for cross-outcome risk-factor combinations, run a two-word PubMed search ("smoking diabetes"); then the same two-word search for risk-factor–risk-factor and source–risk-factor links.

The bar for an arc borrowed two criteria from ESC-DAG (Ferguson 2020): biologically plausible and temporally accurate, with direction set by timing and background knowledge (education precedes adult diet, so education → diet).

The key move is a very permissive inclusion rule: a single peer-reviewed article suggesting an association is enough to draw an arc, because omitting a real arc biases you more than adding a spurious one. Every arc's source is logged in Appendix A, and a second reviewer confirmed each arc.

The genuinely new step is testing the graph. Map the nodes onto the San Luis Valley Diabetes Study (n=1,795), delete the unmeasured environmental-exposure node, have DAGitty emit every implied conditional-independence statement, test each by regression, and count p>0.05 as support.

Then run the same test on 1,000 DAGs with the same skeleton but randomly permuted node labels as a null. The application side uses the single-door criterion (Pearl 2009) to pull a minimal sufficient adjustment set, fit with BKMR, linear mixed effects, and Cox.

Key results

The DAG implies 81 conditional-independence statements, 74 testable, of which 43% are supported by SLVDS (sample sizes 1,265–1,795). The null gives the most striking number: of 1,000 permuted DAGs, 608 had more than 43% supported — so this "evidence-based" DAG beat only about four in ten random graphs and did not outperform chance.

The minimal sufficient adjustment set is identical across all three outcomes: environmental exposure, smoking, diet.

The application findings are secondary: As roughly linear with fasting glucose, Mn U-shaped (under the larger adjustment set); Cd positive with systolic BP under the minimal set but flattened or reversed once environmental-exposure proxies are added (p-trend 0.07), read as confounding by environmental exposure; no metal associated with CVD; no within-mixture interaction.

The abstract lands on one line: evidence for As and Mn with fasting glucose.

How solid is the evidence

The "evidence-based" label is thinner than it sounds. The metal→outcome arcs aren't derived from evidence at all; they're forced in to be tested, and the paper never separates evidence-arcs from design-arcs. The inclusion rule is crude: one article equals one arc, with no weighting by design or quality, and conflicting reviews get an arc anyway (As–CVD is genuinely inconsistent at low doses).

Direction is a judgment call — the authors admit income and alcohol could run either way. Only secondary literature, 42 papers, a single engine, not a systematic review. It's reproducible in that the query and Appendix A are printed, but the node and arc decisions rest on manual plausibility plus a second reviewer: auditable, not mechanized.

The validation is the best idea in the paper, and it failed — the graph lost to roughly 60% of random permutations. The authors' defenses all hold (permuted DAGs may be biologically absurd, e.g. an arc into age; p-only with no effect sizes; the missing environmental-exposure node), but they amount to saying the test as run can't actually certify the DAG.

So we get a construction procedure and a validation idea, neither shown to work on this dataset.

entry.title: Experiment Selection in Meta-Analytic Piecemeal Causal Discovery
entry.content:
Encode the causal claims scattered across many studies (aggregate statistics only, no raw data) as constraints, compute all consistent causal graphs (the equivalence class), then use information gain to pick "the next experiment most worth running." A pre-LLM ancestor of "literature → causal graph."

The authors

From UCLA, the paper joins the groups of senior author Alcino Silva and William Hsu. Silva, a professor of neurobiology and psychiatry, champions "research maps" for integrating knowledge and planning experiments; Hsu works in medical imaging informatics.

The problem

Most of what a scientist reads is free text: papers report aggregate statistics, not raw data — yet classic causal-discovery algorithms run on raw data, and "building causal models with only textual information" has had little work. Biology adds a structural difficulty: you can usually observe only a subset of the system's variables at once (piecemeal), and must stitch subset results together analytically.

The authors concede a hard limit up front: piecemeal causal discovery "often fails to identify the one true causal graph for the system under investigation, regardless of the number of experiments." So the problem splits in two — synthesize causal evidence from many studies into one consistent model, and decide which experiment to run next most efficiently.

The approach

A causal graph G = (V, D), structure only, no parameters. Constraints are written as (conditional) independence relations with an intervention set, X ⊥⊥ Y | C ‖ J (J is the intervention set, J = ∅ is passive observation), bridged by the causal Markov and faithfulness conditions with d-separation linking distributions to graphs.

Solving uses the Hyttinen–Eberhardt–Järvisalo (2014) ASP constraint-based algorithm — which assumes neither acyclicity nor causal sufficiency, so it can represent cycles and latent confounders: conflict-free constraints go to SAT, conflicting ones to weighted MaxSAT, where each constraint carries a confidence weight (from p-values or a research-map edge's evidence score) and the solver finds the graph set minimizing the summed weight of violated constraints.

Evidence comes from Silva's research maps: nodes are phenomena, edges carry a sign (excitation/inhibition/independence) and a methodology tag (↑↓ intervention, ∅↑∅↓ observation), each with a Bayesian aggregate score based on evidential convergence and consistency.

The engine for choosing experiments is the equivalence class and its degrees of freedom (DOF). The consistent-graph set is the equivalence class — the bigger, the more ignorant; DOF is "how many edge-relation variants remain between any pair across the whole class," and since each DAG pair has 3 possibilities (X→Y, X←Y, X···Y), fixing one leaves 2 DOF.

Algorithm 2 picks the pair with the most remaining DOF, then chooses a maximally informative experiment from a lookup, deliberately avoiding uninformative interventions (e.g., if X→Y is among the options, don't intervene on Y — that erases the edge and makes the variants indistinguishable).

Algorithm 3 goes further: for each DOF assignment it computes the expected number of graphs pruned if true, e = (fraction assigning it) × (graphs it would eliminate), and picks the max — a value-of-information objective, but it must enumerate the whole equivalence class, so it costs more.

Algorithm 4 uses just 1–2 SAT queries to sort a proposed hypothesis into three classes: consistent with none (contradiction), consistent with all (already entailed), or consistent with some but not all (the only kind worth testing, guaranteed to prune the class) — which the authors call an analytic expression of Popper's falsifiability.

Key results

A framework plus exhaustive simulation — no new empirical study here (the real two-paper neuroscience merge is in a companion paper). Model-space scale: 1/2/3 variables give 1/3/25 DAGs, 4 variables give 543, 7 variables about 1.1 billion.

The evaluation runs over all 543 four-variable DAGs, each taken as ground truth with an oracle that assumes causal sufficiency and knows the true graph, selecting from the 48 two-variable studies defined by the research-map schema and recording equivalence-class size, averaged across all 543.

Main findings: Algorithm 3 shrinks the class to fewer than 10 graphs (a size a domain expert can review) in 9 experiments vs 19 for random; reaching the minimum, random takes on average 32 more studies than Algorithm 3.

But there's a fundamental ceiling — after every two-variable experiment is done, on average 4–5 graphs remain indistinguishable, the limit of piecemeal two-variable discovery (contrast: with all variables observable and any interventions, log(N)+1 experiments suffice).

How solid is the evidence

Heavily idealized. Although the underlying engine can represent cycles and latent confounders, the demonstrated evaluation is restricted to DAGs (acyclic) with an oracle assuming causal sufficiency (no latent confounders) — so the "general model space" is a property of the tooling, never actually stress-tested by the simulations.

And the oracle returns the correct result for every experiment: no scientist fallibility, no measurement error, no finite-sample independence-test error.

The exhaustive study only reaches N = 4; 7 variables is already ~10⁹, and Algorithm 3 must enumerate the whole class, so large systems are impractical (the fallback is to wait for supercomputers, or drop to the 1–2-SAT-query Algorithm 4 for screening).

The baseline is random experiment selection, which the authors admit doesn't mean real scientists choose at random. Faithfulness, oracle correctness, and the non-identifiability under the two-variable constraint (4–5 residual graphs) are the binding limits.

entry.title: The Burden of Proof studies: assessing the evidence of risk
entry.content:
For each "risk factor → outcome" relationship, fit a conservative lower-bound risk function (BPRF), turn it into an ROS, and compress that into a 1–5 star rating — a reproducible, algorithmic grade of evidence strength. A machine version of GRADE.

The authors

The team is at the Institute for Health Metrics and Evaluation (IHME), University of Washington. Lead author Peng Zheng specializes in statistical modeling and meta-analysis software; senior author Christopher Murray directs IHME and leads the Global Burden of Disease study.

The problem

How strong a risk factor–outcome relationship is gets graded today (GRADE, Cochrane) by expert judgment — poorly reproducible and not comparable across pairs; in the authors' words GRADE is "very helpful, [but] cannot be implemented algorithmically." Conventional dose–response meta-analysis usually imposes log-linearity for tractability and, tellingly, computes between-study heterogeneity but doesn't fold it into the overall uncertainty.

Risk factors with modest effects get waved away with "residual confounding." The authors want a data-driven, reproducible, cross-pair-comparable measure of evidence strength to feed GBD's Comparative Risk Assessment — pitched as a complement to GRADE/Cochrane, not a replacement.

The approach

The core object is the Burden of Proof Risk Function (BPRF): among all risk functions consistent with the data, take the one closest to "no relationship" (RR = 1) — the 5th-percentile curve for harmful risks, the 95th for protective.

The intuition is "even read most conservatively, the effect is at least this big," and it reflects both effect magnitude and uncertainty. The model is MR-BRT (mrtool): a Bayesian, trimmed, mixed-effects spline meta-regression.

Steps: (1) PRISMA extraction, mapping outcomes to GBD causes, imputing unreported exposure intervals.

(2) Estimate the shape with B-splines, no log-linearity assumption, modeling each observation as a ratio of integrals of the spline over the alternative vs reference exposure intervals — which handles inconsistent reference groups and exposure ranges; monotonicity and a max-slope (biological plausibility) constraint enter as linear inequalities on the spline derivatives; a 50-model spline ensemble with randomized knots removes knot sensitivity.

(3) Code six GRADE-style bias domains (population representativeness, exposure, outcome, reverse causation, confounding control, selection bias) as effect modifiers on the signal slope, rank them with Lasso, and add them one at a time under Gaussian priors.

(4) Capture between-study heterogeneity γ with a study-specific random slope; γ is underestimated when studies are few (can hit 0), so take its 95th quantile via the Fisher Information Matrix, and combine that with the fixed-effect posterior for the final UI. (5) Detect publication bias with Egger's regression — flag only, no correction.

(6) Average the log BPRF over the 15th–85th exposure percentile, signed, to get the risk-outcome score (ROS): higher = stronger, ROS < 0 = the conservative reading can't reject the null.

ROS is binned into 1–5 stars.

Key results

Star thresholds (verbatim): ROS < 0 is one star, 0–0.14 two, 0.14–0.41 three, 0.41–0.62 four, > 0.62 five — and those cutpoints are just ln 1.15, ln 1.50, ln 1.85, so harmful and protective are symmetric.

Four worked pairs: smoking→lung cancer, 5 stars (RR 5.11 at 20 pack-years, average risk increase 106.7%, ROS 0.73); SBP→ischemic heart disease, 5 stars (RR 2.38 at 140 vs 100 mmHg, ROS 0.70); vegetable intake→IHD, protective but only 2 stars (RR 0.81 at 100 g/day, average 12.1% reduction, ROS 0.13); unprocessed red meat→IHD, a nominal 2 stars with a BPRF averaging 0.01, essentially on the null (ROS 0.01, only +1.04% risk).

Across GBD's 180 pairs, only 22 (12.2%) earned 4–5 stars while 112 (62.2%) got 1–2. The exposure window is stable: ROS correlates 0.98 when swapped to 10–90 or 5–95.

How solid is the evidence

The key caveat: ROS collapses "evidence strength" and "effect size" onto one axis — red meat gets 2 stars not because the studies are few or weak, but because the conservative lower bound sits at the null.

A five-star rating is not high-quality evidence in the GRADE sense. It means that the conservative bound remains large.

Observational confounding is not resolved. It enters as a coarse study-level bias covariate, and the authors concede that covariate adjustment cannot repair a literature in which most input studies share bias. The analysis stays in the association and dose–response domain, without do-calculus, adjustment-set identification, or a potential-outcomes analysis.

Third, the trimming level (keep the 90% most self-coherent points) is a user-set heuristic; publication bias is detected but not corrected; animal evidence is excluded entirely. The authors list eight limitations — commendably candid.

entry.title: Triangulation in aetiological epidemiology
entry.content:
Triangulation isn't repeating or pooling the same design — it's cross-checking approaches whose key biases are unrelated and ideally point in opposite directions; if they still converge, the causal claim is on firmer ground.

The authors

All three authors are at the MRC Integrative Epidemiology Unit, University of Bristol, known for causal-inference methods in aetiological epidemiology. Lead author Deborah Lawlor is its deputy director, Kate Tilling a professor of medical statistics, and senior author George Davey Smith the unit's director and a Mendelian-randomization pioneer.

The problem

Aetiological epidemiology asks "what causes disease," yet many exposures (breastfeeding, alcohol, the long-term effects of smoking) can't be randomized.

Every single approach carries its own bias; repeating one design, or pooling it by meta-analysis, quietly assumes all the estimates are unbiased and estimating the same effect — but if they're all pulled the same way by the same residual confounding, the "consistent answer" is very likely consistently wrong.

In the authors' words, that similar answer "is likely to be the (residually) confounded answer." What's needed is a framework that systematically exploits the fact that different approaches have different biases, rather than manufacturing false agreement by repetition.

The approach

Their definition, near-verbatim: strengthening causal inference by integrating results from several different "approaches" whose key sources of potential bias differ and are assumed to be largely unrelated. The word "approaches" (not "designs") is deliberate — comparisons can be across designs (RCT vs cohort) and across analytic strategies within one cohort (negative controls, cross-context comparison, MR).

The name comes from 1600s surveying: compute a hard-to-measure distance from a few easy ones plus the geometry of triangles; here the hard-to-measure quantity is an unbiased causal effect, and the "different locations" are approaches with different biases.

The core is a minimum set of five criteria: (1) at least two — ideally more — approaches with differing, unrelated key bias sources are compared; (2) they address the same underlying causal question; (3) the duration and timing of exposure each approach captures is taken into account when comparing; (4) each approach's key bias sources are made explicit; (5) the expected direction of each bias is stated where feasible, ideally with some approaches biased in opposite directions.

Criterion 5 is the crux: if approaches biased in different directions still converge, bias can't easily explain the agreement. A key boundary — throwing many estimators (stratification, PS, IPW, g-formula; or IV, matching, DID) at the same dataset is NOT triangulation, because they share the same measurement error and residual confounding.

What counts as a "bias source" is mechanistic: confounding in observational regression comes from unmeasured confounders, "confounding" in an RCT from subversion of randomization, in MR from population stratification — the same confounding construct, three unrelated sources, so triangulating them is legitimate.

Table 1 lays out each design's assumptions and key bias sources as a knowledge table; Figure 1 uses six IV-DAGs for LDLc→CHD to show that MR on HMGCR variants is mimicking statins, so its pleiotropy path is shared with a statin RCT — those two should not be triangulated, whereas variants acting on a different path give an unrelated violation.

Key results

A methods/opinion piece — the output is a framework, not numbers. The most operationalizable artifacts are Table 1 (design → assumptions → key bias sources) and Figure 1's DAG-based judgment.

The authors are blunt that triangulation today "will mostly provide a qualitative assessment," and that "pointing to the same conclusion does not mean that the results are statistically consistent and could be pooled."

Three deliberately messy examples show the use. Lower SBP→CHD estimates from MR, cohort meta-analysis, and IV ratios from RCTs agree in direction but differ in magnitude; plotting them against exposure duration supports a cumulative-exposure interpretation. Breastfeeding→BMI evidence, including bespoke ALSPAC negative-control outcomes, points toward no causal effect and residual confounding. Maternal glucose→birthweight does not reconcile and ends at "more evidence needed."

How solid is the evidence

The authors are honest about the limits they draw. First, it's qualitative only, with no pooling method — "we have not considered how to pool data in a triangulation framework," and deriving "plausible bounds of causal effect" is explicitly future work.

Second, "are the bias sources unrelated?" is a judgment call that can be wrong — the HMGCR/statin case shows two nominally different designs can share the same bias source.

Third, the direction of bias often can't be predicted a priori; it "cannot be predicted in general," and new biases may only surface once analysis begins. Fourth, they don't claim novelty (it shares roots with Hill's consistency and responses to Duhem–Quine); the contribution is the definition, criteria, and demonstrations.

So it's a program, not a runnable inference engine — and the harder the case, the more it defers to "further evidence."

entry.title: A Pilot Project Leveraging Large Language Models for Automated Screening and Variable Extraction in Observational Studies
entry.content:
Two open-source LLM pipelines: LitScreen screens observational studies, while VarEx extracts exposures, outcomes, and covariates and aligns them to Metaconfoundr. Screening recall is 1.00 in four of five SYNERGY datasets, but production corpora lack complete inclusion gold standards and the manuscript still contains placeholders.

The authors

From the University of New Mexico's Division of Translational Informatics. Senior author Scott A. Malec's lab works on causal inference and literature-based discovery; co-author Melissa L. Rethlefsen, who directs UNM's health sciences library, led the PRISMA-S search-reporting guideline. Manjil M. Pradhan is first author.

The problem

Systematic reviews sit at the top of the evidence hierarchy for chronic-disease causal inference, yet the sheer volume of literature is crushing them: a single review often takes hundreds to over a thousand person-hours and a median of 60-plus weeks from registration to publication, with screening and data extraction the two most labor-intensive steps.

Worse, the observational studies these reviews summarize are wildly inconsistent in how they adjust for confounding — omitting true confounders, or wrongly conditioning on colliders, mediators, instruments — so for the same exposure–outcome pair, different studies adjust for different variables.

The principles exist (VanderWeele's disjunctive cause criterion, DAGs), but reported practice stays all over the map. Existing ML screeners like Abstrackr are mostly proprietary black boxes, weak on transparency and reproducibility.

What's missing is a transparent, open method that both cuts the workload and makes reported exposures, outcomes, and covariates comparable across studies.

The approach

An end-to-end, two-pipeline workflow, entirely on open models (Deepseek-R1:70b, Llama3.3:70b via Ollama) and provider-agnostic.

LitScreen screens in three phases, the idea being "add evidence progressively, spend heavy compute only on the few most uncertain papers." Phase 1 is domain-neutral sentence extraction (llama3.3:70b, few-shot): every specific exposure/outcome/design name is deliberately stripped from the prompt and the few-shot examples so the model can't just keyword-match — it only pulls sentences describing design, exposures, and outcomes.

Phase 2 is criterion-wise adjudication (Deepseek-R1:70b, a reasoning model emitting <think> traces): preliminary read → self-reflection against 4–5 criterion questions → YES/NO/UNCLEAR plus a confidence level, then a deterministic three-way gate — a high-confidence NO excludes immediately, everything else escalates to full text.

Phase 3 is full-text RAG verification: Entrez resolves PMID/PMCID to JATS XML (Unpaywall as fallback), a custom parser keeps only Methods and Results, adaptive chunking (cosine>0.80, 60–500 words), bioLinkBERT-large retrieval → MMR top-50 → bge-reranker top-5 → Deepseek verifies, now barred from UNCLEAR and required to cite a chunk id.

Two anti-hallucination gates carry the design: an evidence-state gate (Disqualifying/Confirming/Insufficient, where a No is only allowed on Disqualifying) and a verbatim gate (the DISQUALIFIER_VERBATIM field must be an exact substring of the extracted sentences, or the No is demoted to Unclear). The whole thing is recall-first — dropping one eligible paper is far worse than keeping one borderline.

VarEx reuses the same parsing and chunking but keeps the abstract too (per STROBE) and pulls tables plus table/figure captions as text.

It embeds with PubMedBERT, retrieves exposure, outcome, and covariate separately with role-specific queries (up to 4 chunks per role), and prompts one role at a time; every extracted variable must carry an exact supporting quote plus chunk id, and the model is told not to generate statistical conclusions.

Covariates are then sorted into 10 semantic categories (sociodemographic, biology/genetics, health behaviors, medication, physiological measurement, psychosocial, clinical symptoms, psychiatric/medical history, underlying conditions, misc), aligned to Metaconfoundr's Confounder Matrix.

Key results

On the primary HTN→ADRD reference set (20 papers, 240 reference covariates): extraction Precision 0.80, Recall 0.79, F1 0.76, classification accuracy 0.97, strict F1 (variable and category both correct) 0.73. Education→Dementia (10 papers) reaches F1 0.81.

Across the SYNERGY validation sets, Bos is best (F1 0.85) and Brouwer (psychological predictors → depression relapse) worst (F1 0.65, classification accuracy just 0.49).

Category-level performance is very uneven: sociodemographics, physiological, and health behaviors all hit ~0.87, while Medication Use collapses to 0.57 (recall 0.47), Underlying Health Conditions is over-extracted (precision 0.60), and a sparse class like Psychosocial sits at 0.33.

On efficiency, against Wang et al.'s manual baseline (abstract 0.9, full-text 7, extraction 53 min/ref, 60.9 total) the pipeline runs 8.68 min/ref, an 85.7% saving.

But read the fine print: abstract screening is actually slower with the LLM (2.95 vs 0.9 min); nearly all the saving comes from data extraction (53→2.53). LitScreen excludes 60.8% of records on average at the abstract stage (94.1% for Education, 86.9% for HTN).

How solid is the evidence

The preprint has inconsistent signs of completion. The body still contains a "MONKEY MONKEY MONKEY TO ADD TO DO" placeholder, the HTN→ADRD PRISMA diagram has several blank n= fields, and a "Things To Do / Incomplete" block remains. Yet later pages already contain the MEDLINE strategy, screening prompt templates, and screening results in Appendix F.

Table 9 reports LitScreen screening performance. BPV→Dementia has precision 0.846 and recall 1.000; recall is 1.000 for Bos, Brouwer, van de Schoot, and Wolters, and 0.725 for van der Valk.

The HTN→ADRD and Education→Dementia production corpora lack complete inclusion labels, so expert review can report precision of 0.857 and 0.655 but cannot estimate accuracy, F1, or recall. High recall is therefore supported in several labeled benchmarks, not yet established in the production corpora.

The extraction evaluation is also generous. Semantic matching counts a true positive at cosine ≥ 0.60, a fairly loose bar that lets near-misses pass; the classification metrics are computed only on already-correctly-extracted covariates, so 0.97 is conditional on that ~79%. More awkwardly, retrieval and evaluation share the same PubMedBERT embedding family, which can turn surface agreement into apparent correctness.

The samples are small: 20 papers in the primary set, a single expert-built extraction standard, no inter-annotator agreement reported for the reference (SYNERGY had two reviewers). Brouwer is consistently the floor, showing psychological/behavioral covariate language is harder — the "generalization" that looks good is really the home turf (HTN, Education).

The efficiency comparison uses a cross-study manual baseline, not a head-to-head on the same corpus, and that 53 min/ref extraction baseline carries most of the 85.7%.

The authors concede hallucination is inevitable (variables not present in the source, decisions on the wrong evidence sentence) and position the tool as an assistant only.

entry.title: Living DAGs: the future of DAGs in epidemiology
entry.content:
DAGs today are used as one-off, study-specific tools and then thrown away. The author argues for turning them into "living," shared causal infrastructure: graded by levels of evidence, versioned, revised as evidence arrives, and built collaboratively, so science can accumulate.

The authors

Robert J. Reynolds is a data scientist for NASA Johnson Space Center's Human Health and Performance Directorate (via contractor KBR) and network scientist for its Human System Risk Board. His work applies causal diagrams and epidemiology to astronaut health risk.

The problem

Over two decades DAGs went from novelty to standard method in epidemiology, used to frame the question, clarify the estimand, find sources of bias, and design the analysis. But the common workflow wastes them: assume the DAG structure is correct (from prior knowledge or the literature), extract the adjustment set, then discard the DAG.

It may appear in a paper, but it is rarely subjected to network analysis beyond d-separation and almost never linked to prior evidence or competing models.

The DAG becomes a study-specific instrument that turns irrelevant once the study ends. The author's point is that this throws away the DAG's potential to support cumulative science.

The approach

This is an opinion / methods call, not an empirical study. The core proposal is to treat DAGs as epistemic infrastructure.
