Living DAGs are not meant to replace the study-specific DAGs used for individual analyses; they are a shared, domain-level causal foundation from which researchers derive their own study-specific DAGs (adding selection nodes, measurement specs, timing) before analysis.

A living DAG has four features: each causal relationship is annotated with a level of evidence (speculative/weak/moderate/strong); it is updated over time as new studies, experiments, or mechanistic models arrive; it is versioned, so you can trace how understanding evolved and why; and it is modular, so parts can be evaluated, revised, or reused independently.

Context differences are handled not by building a separate graph per setting but with modular modifying-factor nodes (geographic, temporal, or demographic nodes that shift pathway strength).

The author separates the universal causal mechanism (the domain DAG) from context-specific magnitudes such as exposure windows: air pollution from three months ago cannot plausibly cause lung cancer today, but exposure from 10 to 20 years ago can; the domain DAG encodes the mechanism, the study-specific DAG specifies the window.

A development-and-revision cycle is sketched (review, propose changes, ratify, release a new version), with competing DAGs allowed to coexist.

Key results

Being an opinion piece, it offers no experimental numbers but two examples already in progress. NASA models human-spaceflight risk with 29 interlinked DAGs, each mapping one risk monitored by the Human System Risk Board, combined into the NASA Human System Risk Network (HSRN), built through structured expert elicitation plus a configuration-review process.

An international cerebral-palsy consortium of more than 50 researchers and clinicians, with input from caregivers and patients, spent two years iteratively building a CP DAG.

Both started from structured expert elicitation and were refined repeatedly. The proposal grew out of a workshop at the 2025 SER meeting, with three related sessions accepted for 2026.

How solid is the evidence

This is a call to action, not a validated method. Its "evidence" is the NASA and CP cases, both products of expert consensus, with no quantification of how much a living DAG improves on the study-specific workflow.

Several hard problems are named but not solved: how to grade evidence levels objectively (who sets the speculative-to-strong boundaries), how to adjudicate competing DAGs, who governs versioning, and how to align incentives and ownership across institutions.

The air-pollution example is intuitive, but the claim that mechanisms are universal while magnitudes are context-specific itself needs empirical support. As a manifesto it is persuasive; as a method it still lacks formalism and validation.

entry.title: Knowledge Graph Embeddings for ICU readmission prediction
entry.content:
Annotate EHR data with biomedical ontologies, embed the resulting knowledge graph, and feed it to classical ML to predict 30-day ICU readmission: AUROC 0.827, AUPRC 0.692, with peak performance reached mid-stay rather than at discharge.

The authors

From the University of Lisbon's LASIGE lab, where senior author Cátia Pesquita leads the health and biomedical informatics line. Her group works on biomedical ontologies and knowledge graphs, applied here to ICU readmission prediction over EHR linked to clinical ontologies.

The problem

ICU readmission is both a health risk for patients (higher mortality, longer stays) and a cost burden for hospitals; in developed countries roughly 1 in 10 discharged ICU patients gets readmitted within the same hospitalization. Predicting readmission risk with ML is not new. The gap is that most methods run directly on raw EHR values and ignore what the data means and how items relate.

The authors' example is blunt: 'Aortic Valve Disease' and 'Coronary Artery Disease' share no similarity under categorical coding, and a string match shares only the uninformative word 'disease', yet both are non-neoplastic heart disorders and sit close together in an ontology hierarchy.

Prior work either used a single ontology or vocabulary covering just one feature type (Lu et al. embed discharge summaries with hyperbolic ICD9 embeddings), or learned graph structure from the EHR itself (Choi et al.), never pulling in external scientific knowledge.

The question here: does ontology-based semantic annotation plus knowledge graph embedding improve readmission prediction.

The approach

A three-stage pipeline. Stage one pulls and cleans data from MIMIC-III; stage two generates semantic features; stage three predicts readmission. The baseline reproduces Lin et al. (the only related work with open code), but the authors add four feature types Lin dropped (prescriptions, initial diagnosis, procedures, lab events) and replace Lin's pre-trained ICD9 word embeddings entirely with KG embeddings.

Three core steps. Ontology selection: BioPortal Recommender scores candidates, NCIT gives the best coverage (about 35%), while MIMIC-III itself carries LOINC for labs, ICD9CM for diagnoses and procedures, NDC for drugs. Annotation: two strategies, one mapping everything to a single ontology (NCIT), one using four ontologies by feature type (NCIT/LOINC/ICD9CM/DRON).

Text annotation uses ElasticSearch to retrieve six candidate classes, then picks the one with the smallest Levenshtein distance.

Embedding: patients become instances linked to the ontology classes that annotate them, forming a KG (6.9M triples single-ontology, 8.3M multi-ontology), embedded via RDF2Vec (Skip-Gram, 500 walks, depth 4) into 300-dimensional vectors.

Multiple annotation vectors within one ontology are summed; across four ontologies they are concatenated into 1,200 dimensions.

Classifiers are deliberately classical, logistic regression, random forest, naive Bayes, SVM, with no hyperparameter tuning, so the contribution of semantic annotation is separated from the choice of algorithm.

A separate timeline design mirrors a real stay: Pre-ICU (demographics plus initial diagnosis), In-ICU (add labs, prescriptions, chart events), Post-ICU (add final diagnosis and procedures), updating risk as information accrues.

Key results

The best combination is single-ontology NCIT + RDF2Vec + random forest, giving In-ICU AUROC 0.827±0.011 and AUPRC 0.692±0.018 under five-fold cross-validation. Against the reproduced baseline (RF AUROC 0.617), that is a gain of over 0.2 AUROC and 0.4 AUPRC. Several findings cut against intuition.

Multiple ontologies performed worse, with all models dropping below 0.5 AUPRC; the authors argue a single semantic space yields a more usable holistic representation.

The timeline is the striking part: In-ICU is the peak, and Post-ICU actually dips slightly once final diagnosis and procedures are added, so discharge-only information adds no incremental value for predicting readmission. Dropping chart events barely changes performance, the real drivers are the lab and prescription embeddings.

Among embedding methods, RDF2Vec and OPA2Vec show no significant difference (Kruskal-Wallis p=0.590/0.207), both beating TransE. The confusion matrix shows the In-ICU model avoids unnecessary readmission for over 90% of patients and prevents premature discharge for about 55% of patients who need to stay, at the cost of unnecessarily prolonging the stay of 10% who did not.

How solid is the evidence

Several caveats. First, the reproduced baseline is clearly below the original (Lin et al. RF 0.712 vs 0.617 here; their LSTM+CNN 0.791, which the authors could not even run due to library incompatibility).

The claim rests on "my gain exceeds their gain," but the two baselines are not on the same footing, so comparing absolute AUROC directly is unfair, the authors concede this.

Second, the label merges "death within 30 days" with "readmission" into the positive class, inherited from Lin et al., but folding death into readmission muddies the clinical reading: the model may mostly be predicting severity rather than whether a patient returns.

Third, everything is on single-center MIMIC-III (2001–2012, one hospital) with no external validation; the ablation showing AUROC stays above 0.7 after randomly dropping 75% of annotations is some evidence of robustness, but cross-institution transfer is untested.

Fourth, no tuning and only classical models is intentional (to isolate the annotation effect), but it also means the reported numbers are not the ceiling this feature set could reach.

On measurement bias, NCIT covers only about 35%, annotation loss is heavy for prescriptions and procedures, and the "multiple ontologies are worse" conclusion may partly reflect annotation quality rather than ontology count.

entry.title: Large language model powered knowledge graph construction for mental health exploration
entry.content:
GPT-4 plus active learning pulls context-tagged triplets from PubMed abstracts, then a dozen databases are fused in, yielding MDKG, a 10M-relation contextualized mental-disorders knowledge graph that cuts expert review time and lifts disease prediction on the UK Biobank.

The authors

This is a multi-institution biostatistics collaboration. Shan Gao is the first author, and Hongtu Zhu is the last author; Kaixian Yu and Yue Yang contributed to the model and data work.

Hongtu Zhu works on UK Biobank-scale imaging genetics and big-data integration. Sheng Yu focuses on medical NLP and biomedical knowledge graphs, while Xueqin Wang works on statistical learning.

The problem

Evidence on mental disorders is distributed across hundreds of thousands of papers and many databases, making comprehensive manual review impractical. High-burden conditions such as schizophrenia and bipolar disorder still lack sufficiently comprehensive public disease-specific knowledge graphs.

Existing biomedical KGs have two recurring limitations. Training data are expensive, which restricts graph scale. Flat triplets also discard conditions such as “women only,” “early-stage MDD,” and study sample size, leaving the same edge difficult to interpret across populations.

The study therefore aimed to increase graph scale while retaining the conditions, populations, provenance, and confidence attached to each relation.

The approach

Two lines: literature extraction, and database fusion.

The literature pipeline combines LLMs with active learning. Keywords were derived from high-burden conditions in GBD 2019, yielding 234,087 PubMed articles. BERN2 and QuickUMLS pre-labeled entities, while GPT-4 added noun phrases for methods and physiological processes. A two-step prompt first determined whether a sentence expressed a target relation and then returned the corresponding entity pair.

Active learning reduced the relation-labeling burden. Candidate sentences were encoded with the extractor's [CLS] token and clustered with weighted K-means, using cross-entropy uncertainty as the weight. Clusters were prioritized by uncertainty and relation-type diversity. Manual refinement of 500 abstracts supplied additional examples for rare relations such as risk.

Production extraction used SpERT.PL with the UMLS-pretrained CODER++ encoder and NoisyTune. GPT-4o then checked entity spans, types, and relations and assigned a score from 1 to 10; outputs below 5 were reprocessed. For contradictory triplets, the full abstract was used to determine whether the conflict represented genuinely different conclusions or an extraction error.

Each literature triplet carries three context layers. These comprise 283,417 conditional statements, seven study-population fields such as age, sex, race, and sample size, and side information such as provenance and confidence.

Population characteristics were extracted from PDF/XML tables using ChatGPT question answering, PaddleOCR, and pdfplumber. The confidence score combined model output, journal impact, cross-source frequency, publication year, and contradiction counts; it supports ranking and review but does not establish relation validity.

The database-fusion pipeline covered DrugBank, DisGeNET, UMLS, MONDO, and other sources. Each source was assessed by origin, generation method, evidence strength, and update frequency before entity alignment with SapBERT embeddings and FAISS retrieval.

Candidate entity pairs above 0.9 cosine similarity were merged. Because crossing this threshold changes node counts, connectivity, and downstream paths, alignment errors directly affect graph structure.

Downstream, MDKG served as external knowledge for prediction in UK Biobank. Entities were mapped through SapBERT to ICD-10 and then to PheCodes, reducing feature dimensionality by about 75%.

RDF2vec generated graph embeddings, and each participant's history was summarized as a 200-dimensional vector. LR, RF, SVM, and XGBoost models predicted MDD, anxiety, and bipolar disorder in 1:2 case-control samples using history from 1–7 years before diagnosis.

Key results

The literature graph contains 236,542 entities and 1,913,461 triplets, with 880,692 unique relations after normalization. After multi-source fusion, MDKG contains 1,642,543 entities and 10,702,976 relations, including nearly one million relations absent from the comparison resources.

For MDD, anxiety, bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, and depression, MDKG contains 5–50 times as much relevant information as PrimeKG, SPOKE, and GENA. NER micro/macro F1 is 0.89/0.88, and joint NER+RE reaches 0.79/0.76 after refinement.

Four experts, including two psychiatrists and two medical PhDs, rated MDKG at 0.79 correctness, slightly above GENA's 0.783. Errors were more often attributed to semantic ambiguity (28%) than factual error (12%).

Context shortened review time. A clinician spent an average of 31 s per MDKG triplet, compared with 129 s for PrimeKG and 85 s for GENA, a 62%–77% reduction. The confidence score correlated 0.78 with human insightfulness ratings.

GENA nevertheless scored higher on insightfulness (2.29 versus 2.18). One explanation offered by the authors is MDKG's inclusion of older textbook-like evidence; 24.34% of its schizophrenia triplets predate 2010.

All three disease-prediction tasks improved after KG embeddings were added. For MDD, XGBoost with EHR+KG reached an AUC of 0.89, compared with 0.82 for EHR alone and 0.71 for KG alone. In the environment-only setting, adding KG information increased SVM AUC from 0.57 to 0.78.

For bipolar disorder, LR increased from 0.70 with KG alone to 0.87 in the integrated model. Overall AUC gains ranged from 0.02 to 0.21 and were larger when the original feature set was sparse. Several embedding dimensions ranked highly in SHAP analysis, although their clinical meaning remains unclear.

How solid is the evidence

Extraction is limited to sentence-level evidence in abstracts; full text, tables, and figures are not systematically covered. Standalone RE F1 is 0.68 and raw joint-extraction F1 is 0.63. The higher post-refinement results depend substantially on GPT-4o, with no independent human recall audit of deleted triplets.

The 0.79 correctness score assesses whether a triplet matches its source sentence. It measures source fidelity rather than the reliability of the source conclusion. Cross-disciplinary rater kappa was 0.04, which weakens interpretation of the point estimate.

The paper's “causal graph” chains risk relations into directed paths with no confounding adjustment, intervention semantics, or counterfactual identification. The eight-step glutamate-to-depression path is suitable for hypothesis generation, not for interpreting an identified causal effect.

Downstream prediction was primarily internal to UK Biobank, whose population is disproportionately White and socially advantaged. External validity is therefore limited. AUC gains varied across models and conditions, the 200-dimensional embeddings lacked clinical interpretability, and synonym or abbreviation errors could still impair entity alignment and graph connectivity.

entry.title: Synthesizing Subject-matter Expertise for Variable Selection in Causal Effect Estimation: A Case Study
entry.content:
Using a known-null effect in real data as a gold standard, the DAG built by first listing outcome prognostic factors controlled confounding best, while forcing in exposure predictors amplified bias.

The authors

Senior author Eleanor J. Murray, an epidemiologist at Boston University, runs the Murray Causal Lab and is known for causal-inference methods and science communication as co-host of the Casual Inference podcast. First author Julia Debertin led this DAG covariate-selection case study.

The problem

Standard advice for choosing covariates in causal inference is to draw a DAG and read off the minimal adjustment set that blocks every confounding path.

The gap is that drawing the DAG itself has almost no actionable, evidence-based guidance: most tutorials cover graph theory or the rules for identifying valid adjustment sets, not which variables to put on the graph or how many generations of ancestors to keep adding. Many epidemiologists report this as a real barrier that keeps them from using DAGs at all.

Several building strategies exist (the disjunctive cause criterion, the "kitchen sink" of all exposure predictors, listing all outcome prognostic factors, evidence synthesis for DAGs), but most have only been checked in simulations, and few studies have compared their confounding control on real data.

The core obstacle is that the "true" DAG in real data is never known, so there is no gold standard to score against.

The approach

The authors exploit a special case: the placebo arm of the Coronary Drug Project trial, where the true causal effect of placebo adherence on 5-year mortality is strongly believed to be null, yet the relationship is heavily confounded by baseline and time-varying covariates (the classic "healthy adherer" bias).

Because the truth is known, it becomes a scoring rule: an adjustment set from a DAG closer to the true structure should yield an estimate closer to null. Starting from the DAG implied by the original 1980 analysis, they sequentially built three variants (baseline and longitudinal versions each, six graphs total):

  • outcome-driven DAG: the 40 mortality prognostic factors the trial experts chose without considering adherence.
  • trimmed DAG: a PubMed scoping review removed variables with no evidence of affecting adherence (most lab and ECG measures), then dropped nodes caused by only one other variable.
  • maximal DAG: following the disjunctive cause criterion, added literature- and expert-suggested adherence/mortality causes (hypertension, depression, alcohol) plus covariate–covariate links.

Analysis used the CDP placebo arm, N=2,410 complete cases. Baseline analysis used the plug-in g-formula; the time-varying analysis used inverse probability weighting for time-varying confounding and censoring, with 95% CIs from 500 bootstraps, plus P value functions to compare each result's compatibility with the true null.

Key results

The unadjusted risk difference was 11 percentage points (95% CI 6.6, 16). With baseline covariates only, all three sets performed similarly and all remained biased (incomplete confounding control): outcome-driven 7.0 (2.9, 11), trimmed 7.6 (3.3, 12), maximal 7.6 (3.4, 12).

Adjusting additionally for time-varying covariates separated them: outcome-driven fell to 2.5 (−2.0, 7.0), closest to null; trimmed 3.7 (−1.1, 8.5); maximal only reached 5.2 (0.4, 10), nearly 3 points further from null with a CI excluding zero.

The P value functions gave the same ranking, outcome-driven most compatible with the true null, maximal largely incompatible. Including non-confounding prognostic factors shrank variance without adding bias, whereas the maximal DAG mostly added variables tied to adherence but weakly to mortality ("near-instruments"), triggering bias amplification.

How solid is the evidence

The strength is the design: using a known-null effect as a ruler sidesteps the "true DAG is unknowable" deadlock, and the conclusions line up with theory (instrument-driven bias amplification, harmlessness of prognostic factors), each corroborating the other. External validity is narrow, though.

It is a single case, a 1970s all-male post-MI cohort; the authors concede the DAGs built for the placebo arm may not even hold for the full trial, let alone other questions.

The adherence measure is coarse, clinicians eyeballed the pill bottle into three bins, few ever fell below 20%, and misclassification is likely, which may blunt the time-varying analysis.

Part of why the maximal DAG failed is data-driven: the depression, cognition, and alcohol variables they wanted were mostly unmeasured, so putting them on the graph yielded no adjustment variable, leaving only the bias-amplifying half. So how much of "maximal is worst" is the method versus an accident of this dataset is hard to separate.

entry.title: Causal knowledge graph analysis identifies adverse drug effects
entry.content:
Mark causal edges on a knowledge graph and it auto-generates "drug mediates two diseases" hypotheses and confounder sets; run mediation analysis on UKB and MIMIC-IV and it both reproduces known adverse reactions and surfaces new candidates.

The authors

From the Bio-Ontology Research Group (BORG) at KAUST, led by corresponding author Robert Hoehndorf, whose group works on biomedical ontologies, knowledge graphs, and machine learning over structured biomedical knowledge. First author Sumyyah Toonsi is also at KAUST; co-author Paul N. Schofield is at the University of Cambridge.

The problem

Rare adverse drug reactions that surface only after a drug ships are often missed in trials because they're infrequent or delayed, so post-marketing surveillance has to catch them.

That task needs two things at once: background knowledge (disease and drug taxonomies, disease progression, indications, known side effects) and co-occurrence frequencies from observational data, plus causal inference to tell a genuine drug effect from a confounded association. Neither existing tool has both ends.

Knowledge graphs organize qualitative knowledge well but carry no probabilistic or causal semantics. Structural causal models estimate intervention effects but sit disconnected from background knowledge, with graphs that are hand-crafted and don't scale. The authors want the two to merge, so the graph itself handles proposing hypotheses, finding confounders, and constraining the distribution.

The approach

Start with a formal definition of a Causal Knowledge Graph (CKG). On top of an ordinary KG it hangs a subset of relations marked causal (Rcausal), a function f mapping each node to a subset of the population, and a probability measure P over the population.

The key move is not equating the KG with the causal graph but making the causal structure a subset of it, so non-causal relations (like the ICD-10 is-a hierarchy) can still drive deductive reasoning and constrain the joint distribution (if u is-a v, any individual in u is in v).

From this they build a Drug-Disease CKG integrating four edge types: disease progression, 7,586 edges (a text-mined causal disease DAG); indications, 20,955 edges (MEDI-C); side effects, 59,119 edges (OnSIDES); and the ICD-10 hierarchy. The causal subset is disease-progression edges plus the inverse of indication edges (having a disease leads to a prescription).

Hypotheses are generated like this.

For each pair x causes-onset y, query x's indicated drug M and assemble the mediation chain "x (indication) → prescribe M → y (potential side effect)." Candidates come from two sources: explicit disease-progression pairs in the CKG (causal set), and significant comorbid pairs in UK Biobank (Hidalgo's relative risk, 34,843 kept after BH correction, then oriented by diagnosis timing).

A SPARQL query then drops hypotheses the graph already explains otherwise, the drug being indicated for both diseases, or both diseases being side effects of the same drug.

That leaves 12,561 causal and 81,610 comorbidity hypotheses.

Statistically it's causal mediation analysis estimating the natural indirect effect (R's mediation package, i.e. ACME), identified under sequential ignorability.

Confounder control is the heaviest part of the paper: beyond demographic and socioeconomic covariates, DAGitty runs the backdoor and disjunctive-cause criteria on the CKG's causal subgraph to pick adjustment sets; comorbidity count and drug count proxy for disease severity; and two LASSOs (one for the indication, one for the outcome) are unioned to shrink the covariate set, with λ from ten-fold CV.

Estimation uses 1,000 quasi-Bayesian simulations, HC standard errors, BH correction.

Key results

On the causal set, "disjunctive cause + LASSO" does best: precision 0.907, recall 0.282, F1 0.431 (versus backdoor+LASSO at F1 0.371). That high precision is partly a freebie, since those edges are literature-backed and mostly already-known effects.

The comorbidity set buys coverage instead: 30,724 significant positive ACMEs, the same recall but precision down to 0.749, more potentially novel candidates. Against Ryan et al.'s expert-curated set (56 overlapping pairs), the comorbidity set hits 70.0% precision and 60.6% recall.

External validation runs through drug repurposing: use predicted side effects to compute drug-drug similarity, then test whether similar drugs share indications. Predicted effects alone give ROCAUC 0.604, below OnSIDES alone at 0.620; but combining the two reaches 0.632 (p≪0.001), so the newly surfaced effects carry information rather than noise.

How solid is the evidence

The crux is sequential ignorability, mediation analysis requires that, after adjusting for confounders, both treatment and mediator are conditionally independent of the potential outcomes, which is untestable in observational data.

The authors admit the worst leak is disease severity: of 900 manually reviewed false positives, about 6% are actually disease progression rather than drug mediation. The mechanism is that a drug is given for an uncontrolled, worsening condition; mild responsive cases never develop the outcome, so the drug "appears" to mediate.

Using comorbidity and drug counts as severity proxies is a blunt instrument, weaker than a weighted index like Charlson. Measurement bias is real too: UKB medication use is self-reported without accurate timing or dose, and ICD diagnoses capture only clinically confirmed phenotypes, so mild or transient effects are invisible.

Both cohorts skew older and socially advantaged, so transportability is doubtful; and with a small, low-overlap gold-standard set, the reported high precision holds only on that small matched slice.

entry.title: Evidence triangulation in health research
entry.content:
No single study settles a question. Stack designs whose weaknesses differ, different assumptions, biases that shouldn't line up, and when they still agree, the conclusion holds. That's evidence triangulation.

The authors

Lead author Sirena Gutierrez is a UC San Francisco epidemiologist from M. Maria Glymour's causal-inference and social-epidemiology group (Glymour now chairs epidemiology at Boston University). Senior author George Davey Smith (University of Bristol) pioneered Mendelian randomization and champions evidence triangulation.

The problem

The perfect study doesn't exist. You can't run one that is large, diverse, randomizes every relevant version of a treatment, and tracks every outcome. The authors open with alcohol and dementia.

Plenty of observational work reports that "moderate drinking" comes with better cognition and less dementia, and the press runs with it every year. But those associations are tangled up with socioeconomic status, regional and religious norms, and past addiction, plus reverse causation (people sliding toward dementia drink less), selective survival, and measurement error. Settle it with one big RCT?

The NIH- and industry-funded MACH trial collapsed midway, and even finished it would have answered one narrow slice. The rest has to come from reading many flawed studies together.

The approach

The core move is to make the biases miss each other: find the worst weakness of each design, then bring in evidence that doesn't share it. The new evidence has its own flaws, but if it rests on different assumptions and its biases don't point the same way, and the two still agree, confidence climbs a level.

The tooling is to sort bias into three buckets (confounding, measurement, selection) and use directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) to lay out the untestable assumptions and pick complementary designs whose identifying assumptions differ.

The authors offer a six-piece toolkit. An RCT is the gold standard but often impossible and answers a narrow slice. Instrumental variables and natural experiments use exogenous, near-arbitrarily-timed variation like a policy change to get around unmeasured confounding, on the same assumption set as an RCT.

Mendelian randomization uses genetic variants, valuable because its assumptions differ completely from conventional confounder control. Negative controls find an exposure with a similar confounding structure but no plausible effect, like using paternal smoking to test the intrauterine mechanism behind maternal smoking and offspring obesity.

Cross-context comparison checks the same association across populations with different confounding.

Quantitative bias analysis simply estimates how large the bias is.

Key results

This is a review, so it offers usable conclusions rather than experimental numbers.

Before combining evidence you align the estimands: for LDL cholesterol to coronary heart disease, an RCT estimates the effect of lowering LDL for five years while MR estimates a genetically set, lifelong trajectory, so the MR effect is much larger, but the RCT runs about 40% of MR, a stable ratio you can rescale.

Exposure and outcome definitions need calibrating too (drinks per day versus week, 4+ versus 5+ for a binge, all-cause versus clinically diagnosed dementia).

Often the heterogeneity teaches you more than a single pooled effect; when the direction of bias is clear, an upward-biased and a downward-biased estimate bracket the truth.

How solid is the evidence

This is a methods review, so what you weigh is how mature the framework itself is. The quantitative-combination methods are still developing, and most of the time you can only compare qualitatively. Cross-context and IV work both need large samples, while existing data skews toward high-income, socially advantaged populations.

Triangulation can be gamed too: reporting only the negative control that fits, telling the story after the fact, publication bias. The authors' answer is running the analysis across many reasonable specifications, plus pre-registering the triangulation plan.
